From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Historical sovereign state in Northwestern Europe (1801–1922)

This article is about the state from 1801 to 1922. For the current state, see [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom"). For the archipelago, see [British Isles](/wiki/British_Isles "British Isles").

The **United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland** was established by the [Acts of Union](/wiki/Acts_of_Union_1800 "Acts of Union 1800") in 1801 that united the [Kingdom of Great Britain](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Great_Britain "Kingdom of Great Britain") and the [Kingdom of Ireland](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Ireland "Kingdom of Ireland") into one [sovereign state](/wiki/Sovereign_state "Sovereign state").[[4]](#cite_note-9) It continued in this form until 1927, when it evolved into the [United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland](/wiki/United_Kingdom_of_Great_Britain_and_Northern_Ireland "United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland"), after the [Irish Free State](/wiki/Irish_Free_State "Irish Free State") gained a degree of independence in 1922.

[Rapid industrialisation](/wiki/Economic_history_of_the_United_Kingdom "Economic history of the United Kingdom") that began in the decades prior to the state's formation continued up until the mid-19th century. The [Great Irish Famine](/wiki/Great_Irish_Famine "Great Irish Famine"), exacerbated by government inaction in the mid-19th century, led to [demographic collapse](/wiki/Societal_collapse "Societal collapse") in much of Ireland and increased calls for [Irish land reform](/wiki/Land_Acts_(Ireland) "Land Acts (Ireland)"). The 19th century was an era of [Industrial Revolution](/wiki/Industrial_Revolution "Industrial Revolution"), and growth of trade and finance, in which Britain largely dominated the [world economy](/wiki/World_economy "World economy"). Outward migration was heavy to the principal British overseas possessions and to the United States.

The UK, from [its islands](/wiki/British_Isles "British Isles") off the coast of Europe, financed the coalition that defeated France during the [Napoleonic Wars](/wiki/Napoleonic_Wars "Napoleonic Wars"), and developed its dominant [Royal Navy](/wiki/Royal_Navy "Royal Navy") enabling the [British Empire](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire") to become the foremost [world power](/wiki/Superpower "Superpower") for the next century. From the defeat of [Napoleon](/wiki/Napoleon "Napoleon") to [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I"), Britain was [almost continuously at peace with the Great Powers](/wiki/International_relations_(1814%E2%80%931919) "International relations (1814–1919)").[[5]](#cite_note-10) However, the UK did engage in extensive wars in Africa and Asia, such as the [Opium Wars](/wiki/Opium_Wars "Opium Wars"), to extend its empire and influence. The [Colonial Office](/wiki/Colonial_Office "Colonial Office") and [India Office](/wiki/India_Office "India Office") ruled through a small number of administrators who managed the units of the empire locally, while local institutions developed. [British India](/wiki/British_Raj "British Raj") was by far the most important overseas possession. In overseas policy, the central policy was [free trade](/wiki/Free_trade "Free trade"), which enabled British financiers and merchants to operate successfully in otherwise independent countries, as in South America. Beginning in earnest in the second half of the 19th century, the Imperial government granted increasing autonomy to local governments in colonies where white settlers were politically dominant, with this process resulting in [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada"), [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia"), [New Zealand](/wiki/Dominion_of_New_Zealand "Dominion of New Zealand"), [Newfoundland](/wiki/Dominion_of_Newfoundland "Dominion of Newfoundland") and [South Africa](/wiki/Union_of_South_Africa "Union of South Africa") becoming self-governing [dominions](/wiki/Dominion "Dominion"). While these remained part of the Empire, they were permitted greater management of their internal affairs, with Britain remaining responsible for their foreign and trade policies.

With respect to other powers, the British [remained non-aligned](/wiki/Splendid_isolation "Splendid isolation") until the 20th century when the growing naval power of the [German Empire](/wiki/German_Empire "German Empire") came to be seen as an existential threat to the British Empire. In response, London began to cooperate with [Japan](/wiki/Empire_of_Japan "Empire of Japan"), [France](/wiki/French_Third_Republic "French Third Republic") and Russia, and moved closer to the United States. Although not formally allied with any of these powers, by 1914 British policy had all but committed to declaring war on Germany if the latter attacked France. This was realized in 1914 when Germany invaded France via [Belgium](/wiki/Belgium "Belgium"), whose neutrality had been guaranteed by London. The ensuing First World War pitted the [Allied and Associated Powers](/wiki/Allied_and_Associated_Powers "Allied and Associated Powers") including the British Empire, France, Russia, [Italy](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Italy "Kingdom of Italy") and the U.S. against the [Central Powers](/wiki/Central_Powers "Central Powers") of Germany, [Austria-Hungary](/wiki/Austria-Hungary "Austria-Hungary") and the [Ottoman Empire](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire"). The war ended in an Allied victory in 1918 but inflicted a massive cost to British manpower, materiel and treasure.

Growing desire for [Irish self-governance](/wiki/Irish_Home_Rule_movement "Irish Home Rule movement") led to the [Irish War of Independence](/wiki/Irish_War_of_Independence "Irish War of Independence"), which resulted in British recognition of the [Irish Free State](/wiki/Irish_Free_State "Irish Free State") in 1922. Although the Free State was explicitly governed under dominion status and thus was not a fully independent polity, as a dominion it was no longer part of the United Kingdom and ceased to be represented in the [Westminster Parliament](/wiki/Westminster_Parliament "Westminster Parliament"). [Six northeastern counties](/wiki/Northern_Ireland "Northern Ireland") in Ireland, which since 1920 were being governed under a [more limited form of home rule](/wiki/Government_of_Ireland_Act_1920 "Government of Ireland Act 1920"), opted-out of joining the Free State and remained part of the Union. In light of these changes, the British state was renamed the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland on 12 April 1927 with the [Royal and Parliamentary Titles Act](/wiki/Royal_and_Parliamentary_Titles_Act "Royal and Parliamentary Titles Act"). The modern-day United Kingdom is the same state, a direct continuation of what remained after the Irish Free State's secession, as opposed to being an entirely new [successor state](/wiki/Successor_state "Successor state").[[6]](#cite_note-11)

## 1801 to 1820

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_Kingdom_of_Great_Britain_and_Ireland&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: 1801 to 1820")]

Further information: [Georgian era](/wiki/Georgian_era "Georgian era"), [Regency era](/wiki/Regency_era "Regency era"), and [Georgian society](/wiki/Georgian_society "Georgian society")

### Union of Great Britain and Ireland

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_Kingdom_of_Great_Britain_and_Ireland&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Union of Great Britain and Ireland")]

Main article: [Acts of Union 1800](/wiki/Acts_of_Union_1800 "Acts of Union 1800")

A brief period of limited independence for the [Kingdom of Ireland](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Ireland "Kingdom of Ireland") came to an end following the [Irish Rebellion of 1798](/wiki/Irish_Rebellion_of_1798 "Irish Rebellion of 1798"), which occurred during the [British war with revolutionary France](/wiki/French_Revolutionary_Wars "French Revolutionary Wars"). The [Kingdom of Great Britain](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Great_Britain "Kingdom of Great Britain")'s fear of an independent Ireland siding against them with [Revolutionary France](/wiki/French_First_Republic "French First Republic") resulted in the decision to unite the two countries. This was brought about by [legislation in the parliaments of both kingdoms](/wiki/Acts_of_Union_1800 "Acts of Union 1800") and came into effect on 1 January 1801. The Irish had been led to believe by the British that their loss of legislative independence would be compensated with [Catholic emancipation](/wiki/Catholic_emancipation "Catholic emancipation"), that is, by the removal of [civil disabilities placed upon Roman Catholics](/wiki/Civil_disabilities_placed_upon_Roman_Catholics "Civil disabilities placed upon Roman Catholics") in both [Great Britain](/wiki/Great_Britain "Great Britain") and [Ireland](/wiki/Ireland "Ireland"). Despite personal sympathy for Roman Catholics, [King George III](/wiki/King_George_III "King George III") believed that agreeing to Catholic Emancipation would violate his Coronation Oath to uphold the Protestant faith, and his lack of support for the initiative led the Prime Minister, [William Pitt the Younger](/wiki/William_Pitt_the_Younger "William Pitt the Younger"), to resign.[[7]](#cite_note-12)

### Napoleonic Wars

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_Kingdom_of_Great_Britain_and_Ireland&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Napoleonic Wars")]

Further information: [Napoleonic Wars](/wiki/Napoleonic_Wars "Napoleonic Wars") and [British Army during the Napoleonic Wars](/wiki/British_Army_during_the_Napoleonic_Wars "British Army during the Napoleonic Wars")

During the [War of the Second Coalition](/wiki/War_of_the_Second_Coalition "War of the Second Coalition") (1799–1801), Britain occupied most of the [French](/wiki/French_colonial_empire "French colonial empire") and [Dutch overseas possessions](/wiki/Dutch_Empire "Dutch Empire"), the Netherlands having become a [satellite state](/wiki/Satellite_state "Satellite state") of France in 1796, but [tropical diseases](/wiki/Tropical_disease "Tropical disease") claimed the lives of over 40,000 troops. When the [Treaty of Amiens](/wiki/Treaty_of_Amiens "Treaty of Amiens") ended the war, Britain agreed to return most of the territories it had seized. The peace settlement was in effect only a ceasefire, and Napoleon continued to provoke the British by attempting a trade embargo on the country and by occupying the city of [Hanover](/wiki/Hanover "Hanover"), capital of the [Electorate](/wiki/Electorate_of_Hanover "Electorate of Hanover"), a German-speaking duchy of the [Holy Roman Empire](/wiki/Holy_Roman_Empire "Holy Roman Empire") which was in a [personal union](/wiki/Personal_union "Personal union") with the United Kingdom. In May 1803, war was declared again. [Napoleon's plans to invade Great Britain](/wiki/Napoleon%27s_planned_invasion_of_the_United_Kingdom "Napoleon's planned invasion of the United Kingdom") failed, chiefly due to the inferiority of his navy, and in 1805 a [Royal Navy](/wiki/Royal_Navy "Royal Navy") fleet led by [Nelson](/wiki/Horatio_Nelson,_1st_Viscount_Nelson "Horatio Nelson, 1st Viscount Nelson") decisively defeated the [French Imperial Navy](/wiki/French_Imperial_Navy "French Imperial Navy") and [Royal Spanish Navy](/wiki/Royal_Spanish_Navy "Royal Spanish Navy") at [Trafalgar](/wiki/Battle_of_Trafalgar "Battle of Trafalgar"), which was the last significant naval action of the Napoleonic Wars.[[8]](#cite_note-13)

In 1806, Napoleon issued the series of [Berlin Decrees](/wiki/Berlin_Decree "Berlin Decree"), which brought into effect the [Continental System](/wiki/Continental_System "Continental System"). This policy aimed to eliminate the threat from the British by closing French-controlled territory to foreign trade. The [British Army](/wiki/British_Army "British Army") remained a minimal threat to France; it maintained a standing strength of just 220,000 men at the height of the Napoleonic Wars, whereas the [French Imperial Army](/wiki/French_Imperial_Army "French Imperial Army") exceeded a million men—in addition to the armies of numerous allies and several hundred thousand [national guardsmen](/wiki/National_Guard_(France) "National Guard (France)") that Napoleon could draft into the French armies when they were needed. Although the Royal Navy effectively disrupted France's extra-continental trade—both by seizing and threatening French shipping and by seizing French colonial possessions—it could do nothing about France's trade with the major continental economies and posed little threat to French territory in Europe. France's population and agricultural capacity far outstripped that of the [British Isles](/wiki/British_Isles "British Isles"), but it was smaller in terms of industry, finance, mercantile marine and naval strength.[[9]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKnight2015-14)

[Napoleon](/wiki/Napoleon "Napoleon") expected that cutting Britain off from [Continental Europe](/wiki/Continental_Europe "Continental Europe") would end its economic hegemony. On the contrary Britain possessed the greatest industrial capacity in the world, and its mastery of the seas allowed it to build up considerable economic strength through trade to its possessions and the United States. The [Spanish uprising](/wiki/Dos_de_Mayo_Uprising "Dos de Mayo Uprising") in 1808 at last permitted Britain to gain a foothold on the Continent. The [Duke of Wellington](/wiki/Duke_of_Wellington "Duke of Wellington") gradually pushed the French out of Spain, and in early 1814, as Napoleon was being driven back in the east by the [Royal Prussian Army](/wiki/Royal_Prussian_Army "Royal Prussian Army"), the [Imperial Austrian Army](/wiki/Imperial_Austrian_Army_(1806%E2%80%931867) "Imperial Austrian Army (1806–1867)"), and the [Imperial Russian Army](/wiki/Imperial_Russian_Army "Imperial Russian Army"), Wellington invaded southern France. After Napoleon's surrender and exile to the [Principality of Elba](/wiki/Principality_of_Elba "Principality of Elba"), peace appeared to have returned. Napoleon suddenly reappeared in 1815. The Allies united and the armies of Wellington and [Gebhard Leberecht von Blücher](/wiki/Gebhard_Leberecht_von_Bl%C3%BCcher "Gebhard Leberecht von Blücher") defeated Napoleon once and for all at the [Battle of Waterloo](/wiki/Battle_of_Waterloo "Battle of Waterloo").[[10]](#cite_note-15)

### War of 1812 with the United States

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_Kingdom_of_Great_Britain_and_Ireland&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: War of 1812 with the United States")]

Main articles: [War of 1812](/wiki/War_of_1812 "War of 1812") and [Napoleonic Wars](/wiki/Napoleonic_Wars "Napoleonic Wars")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d0/Am%C3%A9d%C3%A9e_Forestier_-_Signing_of_Treaty_of_Ghent_%281814%29.jpg/250px-Am%C3%A9d%C3%A9e_Forestier_-_Signing_of_Treaty_of_Ghent_%281814%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Am%C3%A9d%C3%A9e_Forestier_-_Signing_of_Treaty_of_Ghent_(1814).jpg)

The signing of the [Treaty of Ghent](/wiki/Treaty_of_Ghent "Treaty of Ghent") ending the war with the United States  
(by [Amédée Forestier](/wiki/Am%C3%A9d%C3%A9e_Forestier "Amédée Forestier"), c. 1915)

To defeat France, Britain put heavy pressure on the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States"), seizing merchant ships suspected of trading with France, and impressing sailors (conscription) born in Britain, regardless of their claimed [American citizenship](/wiki/American_citizenship "American citizenship"). British government agents armed [Indigenous American](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas "Indigenous peoples of the Americas") tribes in Canada that were raiding American settlements on the [frontier](/wiki/American_frontier "American frontier"). The Americans felt humiliated and demanded war to restore their honour, despite their complete unpreparedness. The [War of 1812](/wiki/War_of_1812 "War of 1812") was a minor sideshow to the British, but the American army performed very poorly, and was unable to successfully attack Canada. In 1813, the Americans took control of [Lake Erie](/wiki/Lake_Erie "Lake Erie") and thereby of [western Ontario](/wiki/Western_Ontario "Western Ontario"), knocking most of the Indian tribes out of the war. When Napoleon surrendered for the first time in 1814, three separate forces were sent to attack the Americans in [upstate New York](/wiki/Upstate_New_York "Upstate New York"), along the [Maryland](/wiki/Maryland "Maryland") coast (burning [Washington](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C.") but getting repulsed at [Baltimore](/wiki/Baltimore "Baltimore")), and up the [Mississippi River](/wiki/Mississippi_River "Mississippi River") to a massive defeat at the [Battle of New Orleans](/wiki/Battle_of_New_Orleans "Battle of New Orleans"). Each operation proved a failure with the British commanding generals killed or in disgrace. The war was a stalemate without purpose. A negotiated peace was reached at the end of 1814 that restored the prewar boundaries. British Canada celebrated its deliverance from American rule, Americans celebrated victory in a "second war of independence," and Britain celebrated its defeat of Napoleon. The treaty opened up two centuries of peace and open borders.[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBlack2009-16)

### Postwar reaction: 1815–1822

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_Kingdom_of_Great_Britain_and_Ireland&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Postwar reaction: 1815–1822")]

Britain emerged from the Napoleonic Wars a very different country than it had been in 1793. As the [Industrial Revolution](/wiki/Industrial_Revolution "Industrial Revolution") progressed, society changed, becoming more urban. The postwar period saw an economic slump, and poor harvests and inflation caused widespread social unrest. British leadership was intensely conservative, ever watchful of signs of revolutionary activity of the sort that had so deeply affected France. Historians have found very few signs, noting that social movements such as [Methodism](/wiki/Methodist_Church_of_Great_Britain "Methodist Church of Great Britain") strongly encouraged conservative support for the political and social status quo.[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETombs2014455–458-17)

The major constitutional changes included a reform of Parliament, and a sharp decline in the power and prestige of the monarchy. The Prince regent, on becoming [King George IV](/wiki/King_George_IV "King George IV") in 1820 asked Parliament to divorce his wife Queen [Caroline of Brunswick](/wiki/Caroline_of_Brunswick "Caroline of Brunswick") so that he could marry his favourite lover. Public and elite opinion strongly favoured the Queen and ridiculed the king. The fiasco helped ruin the prestige of the monarchy and it recovered a fraction of the power wielded by King [George III](/wiki/George_III "George III") in his saner days. Historian Eugene Black says:

:   the damage was irrevocable. The sovereign was increasingly a symbolic contradiction in his own age. Through madness, stupidity, and immorality Victoria's three predecessors lowered the stock of monarchy. Only thirty years of the narrow domestic virtues of [Queen Victoria](/wiki/Queen_Victoria "Queen Victoria") finally retrieved the symbolic luster of the sovereign.[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBlack196932-18)

#### Ultra Tories: Peterloo Massacre and the Six Acts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_Kingdom_of_Great_Britain_and_Ireland&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Ultra Tories: Peterloo Massacre and the Six Acts")]

Main articles: [Ultra-Tories](/wiki/Ultra-Tories "Ultra-Tories"), [Peterloo Massacre](/wiki/Peterloo_Massacre "Peterloo Massacre"), and [Six Acts](/wiki/Six_Acts "Six Acts")

The [Ultra-Tories](/wiki/Ultra-Tories "Ultra-Tories") were the leaders of reaction and seemed to dominate the [Tory Party](/wiki/Tory_Party "Tory Party"), which controlled the government.[[14]](#cite_note-19) Every untoward event seemed to point to a conspiracy on the left which necessitated more repression to head off another terror such as happened in the [French Revolution](/wiki/French_Revolution "French Revolution") in 1793. Historians find that the violent radical element was small and weak; there were a handful of small conspiracies involving men with few followers and careless security; they were quickly suppressed.[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEvans2008[httpsbooksgooglecombooksidZmjJAwAAQBAJpgPA3_3–25]-20) Nevertheless, techniques of repression included the suspension of [Habeas Corpus in 1817](/wiki/Habeas_Corpus_Act_1816 "Habeas Corpus Act 1816") (allowing the government to arrest and hold suspects without cause or trial). [Sidmouth](/wiki/Henry_Addington "Henry Addington")'s [Gagging Acts](/wiki/Gagging_Acts "Gagging Acts") of 1817 heavily muzzled the opposition newspapers; the reformers switched to pamphlets and sold 50,000 a week.[[16]](#cite_note-21)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/98/Peterloo_Massacre.png/250px-Peterloo_Massacre.png)](/wiki/File:Peterloo_Massacre.png)

The [Peterloo Massacre](/wiki/Peterloo_Massacre "Peterloo Massacre") of 1819 resulted in 18 deaths and several hundred injured.

In industrial districts in 1819, factory workers demanded better wages, and demonstrated. The most important event was the [Peterloo Massacre](/wiki/Peterloo_Massacre "Peterloo Massacre") in [Manchester](/wiki/Manchester "Manchester"), on 16 August 1819, when a local militia unit composed of landowners charged into an orderly crowd of 60,000 which had gathered to demand the reform of parliamentary representation. The crowd panicked and eleven died and hundreds were injured. The government saw the event as an opening battle against revolutionaries. In reaction [Lord Liverpool](/wiki/Lord_Liverpool "Lord Liverpool")'s government passed the "[Six Acts](/wiki/Six_Acts "Six Acts")" in 1819. They prohibited drills and military exercises; facilitated warrants for the search for weapons; outlawed public meetings of more than 50 people, including meetings to organise petitions; put heavy penalties on blasphemous and seditious publications; imposing a fourpenny stamp act on many pamphlets to cut down the flow on news and criticism. Offenders could be harshly punished including [exile in Australia](/wiki/Convicts_in_Australia "Convicts in Australia"). In practice the laws were designed to deter troublemakers and reassure conservatives; they were not often used.[[17]](#cite_note-22)

One historian would write: "Peterloo was a blunder; it was hardly a massacre." It was a serious mistake by local authorities who did not understand what was happening.[[18]](#cite_note-23) Nevertheless, it had a major impact on British opinion at the time and on history ever since as a symbol of officialdom brutally suppressing a peaceful demonstration thinking mistakenly that it was the start of an insurrection.[[19]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBriggs1959208–214-24)

The Ultra-Tories peaked in strength about 1819–1822 then lost ground inside the Tory Party. They were defeated in important breakthroughs that took place in the late 1820s in terms of tolerating first dissenting Protestants.[[20]](#cite_note-25) An even more decisive blow was the unexpected repeal of the many restrictions on Catholics, after widespread organised protest by the [Catholic Association](/wiki/Catholic_Association "Catholic Association") in Ireland under [Daniel O'Connell](/wiki/Daniel_O%27Connell "Daniel O'Connell"), with support from [Catholics in England](/wiki/Catholic_Church_in_England_and_Wales "Catholic Church in England and Wales").[[21]](#cite_note-26) [Robert Peel](/wiki/Robert_Peel "Robert Peel") was alarmed at the strength of the Catholic Association, warning in 1824, "We cannot tamely sit by while the danger is hourly increasing, while a power co-ordinate with that of the Government is rising by its side, nay, daily counteracting its views."[[22]](#cite_note-27) The Duke of Wellington, Britain's most famous war hero, told Peel, "If we cannot get rid of the Catholic Association, we must look to Civil War in Ireland sooner or later."[[23]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPeel1853348-28) Peel and Wellington agreed that to stop the momentum of the Catholic Association it was necessary to pass [Catholic emancipation](/wiki/Catholic_emancipation "Catholic emancipation"), which gave Catholics the vote and the right to sit in Parliament. That happened in 1829 using Whig support. Passage demonstrated that the veto power long held by the ultra-Tories no longer was operational, and significant reforms were now possible across the board. The stage was set for the Age of Reform.[[24]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHilton2006384–391,_668–671-29)

## Age of Reform: 1820–1837

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_Kingdom_of_Great_Britain_and_Ireland&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Age of Reform: 1820–1837")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/85/Trafalgar_Square_by_James_Pollard.jpg/250px-Trafalgar_Square_by_James_Pollard.jpg)](/wiki/File:Trafalgar_Square_by_James_Pollard.jpg)

A painting by [James Pollard](/wiki/James_Pollard "James Pollard") showing [Trafalgar Square](/wiki/Trafalgar_Square "Trafalgar Square") before the erection of [Nelson's Column](/wiki/Nelson%27s_Column "Nelson's Column")

The era of reform came in a time of peace, guaranteed in considerable part by the overwhelming power of the Royal Navy. Britain engaged in only one serious war between 1815 and 1914, the [Crimean War](/wiki/Crimean_War "Crimean War") against the [Russian Empire](/wiki/Russian_Empire "Russian Empire") in the 1850s. That war was strictly limited in terms of scope and impact. The major result was the realisation that military medical services needed urgent reform, as advocated by the nursing leader [Florence Nightingale](/wiki/Florence_Nightingale "Florence Nightingale"). British diplomats, led by [Lord Palmerston](/wiki/Lord_Palmerston "Lord Palmerston"), promoted British nationalism, opposed reactionary regimes on the continent, helped the [Spanish colonies](/wiki/Spanish_colonization_of_the_Americas "Spanish colonization of the Americas") to free themselves and worked to shut down the international slave trade.[[25]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWoodward1962-30)

It was a time of prosperity, population growth and better health, except in Ireland where over one million deaths were caused by the [Great Famine](/wiki/Great_Famine_(Ireland) "Great Famine (Ireland)") when the potato crop failed in the 1840s. The Government did little to help the starving poor in Ireland. Along with the one million deaths, another one million would [emigrate](/wiki/Irish_diaspora "Irish diaspora") in a few short years, mostly to Britain and to the United States. The trend of emigration would continue in Ireland for decades and Ireland's population has never recovered to its pre-famine levels. The [Irish language](/wiki/Irish_language "Irish language") was almost wiped out. The failure of the British government to respond to the crisis in the eyes of the [Irish people](/wiki/Irish_people "Irish people") would lead to a growth in [resentment of Britain](/wiki/Anti-British_sentiment "Anti-British sentiment") and a rise in [Irish nationalism](/wiki/Irish_nationalism "Irish nationalism"). The famine is remembered in Ireland to this day as oppression by the [British Empire](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[Industrial Revolution](/wiki/Industrial_Revolution "Industrial Revolution") accelerated, with [textile mills](/wiki/Textile_manufacture_during_the_British_Industrial_Revolution "Textile manufacture during the British Industrial Revolution") joined by iron and steel, coal mining, railroads and shipbuilding. The second British Empire, founded after the loss of the [Thirteen Colonies](/wiki/Thirteen_Colonies "Thirteen Colonies") in the [American Revolutionary War](/wiki/American_Revolutionary_War "American Revolutionary War") of the 1770s, was dramatically expanded in [India](/wiki/British_Raj "British Raj"), other parts of Asia, and Africa. There was little friction with other colonial powers until the 1890s. British foreign policy avoided entangling alliances.[[26]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBriggs1959-31)

Britain from the 1820s to the 1860s experienced a turbulent and exciting "age of reform". The century started with 15 years of war against France, ending in Wellington's triumph against Napoleon in 1815 at Waterloo. There followed 15 difficult years, in which the Tory Party, representing a small, rich [landed aristocracy](/wiki/Landed_aristocracy "Landed aristocracy") that was fearful of a popular revolution along the French model, employed severe repression. In the mid-1820s, however, as popular unrest increased, the government made a series of dramatic changes. The more [liberal](/wiki/Liberalism_in_the_United_Kingdom "Liberalism in the United Kingdom") among the Tories rejected the [ultraconservative](/wiki/Far-right_politics "Far-right politics") "Ultra Tory" faction. The party split, key leaders switched sides, the Tories lost power, and the more liberally minded opposition [Whigs](/wiki/Whigs_(British_political_party) "Whigs (British political party)") took over. The Tory coalition fell apart, and it was reassembled under the banner of the Conservative Party. Numerous Tories, such as [Lord Palmerston](/wiki/Lord_Palmerston "Lord Palmerston"), switched over to the Whig opposition, and it became the [Liberal Party](/wiki/Liberal_Party_(UK) "Liberal Party (UK)").[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEvans2008[httpsbooksgooglecombooksidZmjJAwAAQBAJpgPA69_69–75]-32)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c7/Opening_of_the_Liverpool_and_Manchester_Railway.jpg/250px-Opening_of_the_Liverpool_and_Manchester_Railway.jpg)](/wiki/File:Opening_of_the_Liverpool_and_Manchester_Railway.jpg)

[Opening of the Liverpool and Manchester Railway](/wiki/Opening_of_the_Liverpool_and_Manchester_Railway "Opening of the Liverpool and Manchester Railway") in 1830

Constitutionally, the 1830s marks a watershed: the end of Crown control over the cabinet. King [William IV](/wiki/William_IV "William IV") in 1834 was obliged to accept a [Prime Minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_the_United_Kingdom "Prime Minister of the United Kingdom") who had a majority in Parliament, and the Crown ever since has gone along with the majority.[[28]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEvans1996257–258-33)

The great [Reform Act 1832](/wiki/Reform_Act_1832 "Reform Act 1832") came at a time of intense public and elite anxiety and broke the logjam. The parliamentary system, based on a very small electorate and large numbers of seats that were tightly controlled by a small elite, was radically reformed. For the first time the growing industrial cities had representation in Parliament. This opened the way for another decade of reform that culminated in the repeal of the [Corn Laws](/wiki/Corn_Laws "Corn Laws") in 1846—ending the tariff on imported grain that kept prices high for the landed aristocracy. Repeal was heavily promoted by the [Anti-Corn Law League](/wiki/Anti-Corn_Law_League "Anti-Corn Law League"), grass roots activists led by [Richard Cobden](/wiki/Richard_Cobden "Richard Cobden") and based in the industrial cities; they demanded cheap food. There were a series of reforms of the electoral laws, expanding the number of male voters and reducing the level of corruption. The reactionary Tory element was closely linked to the [Church of England](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England"), and expressed its strong hostility toward Catholics and nonconformist Protestants by restricting their political and civil rights. The Catholic started to organise in Ireland, threatening instability or even civil war, and the moderates in Parliament emancipated them. The Nonconformists were similarly freed from their restrictions. In addition to reforms at the Parliamentary level, there was a reorganisation of the governmental system in the rapidly growing cities, putting a premium on modernisation and expertise, and large electorates as opposed to small ruling cliques. A rapidly growing middle class, as well as active intellectuals, broaden the scope of reform to include humanitarian activities such as a new poor law and factory laws to protect women and children workers.[[29]](#cite_note-34)

### Protestant Nonconformists

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_Kingdom_of_Great_Britain_and_Ireland&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Protestant Nonconformists")]

Main article: [Nonconformist (Protestantism)](/wiki/Nonconformist_(Protestantism) "Nonconformist (Protestantism)")

In the 1790–1815 period there was an improvement in morals caused by the religious efforts by evangelicals inside the Church of England,[[30]](#cite_note-35) and Dissenters or Nonconformist Protestants as people:

> became wiser, better, more frugal, more honest, more respectable, more virtuous, than they ever were before." Wickedness still flourished, but the good were getting better, as frivolous habits were discarded for more serious concerns. The leading moralist of the era, [William Wilberforce](/wiki/William_Wilberforce "William Wilberforce"), saw everywhere "new proofs presenting themselves of the diffusion of religion".[[31]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBriggs1959175-36)

Nonconformists, including [Presbyterians](/wiki/Presbyterians "Presbyterians"), [Congregationalists](/wiki/Congregationalists "Congregationalists"), the [Baptists](/wiki/Baptists "Baptists") and the rapidly-growing [Methodist denomination](/wiki/Methodism "Methodism"), as well as [Quakers](/wiki/Quakers "Quakers"), [Unitarians](/wiki/General_Assembly_of_Unitarian_and_Free_Christian_Churches "General Assembly of Unitarian and Free Christian Churches") and smaller groups.[[32]](#cite_note-37) They were all outside the established Church of England (except in Scotland, where the established [Church of Scotland](/wiki/Church_of_Scotland "Church of Scotland") was [Presbyterian](/wiki/Presbyterian "Presbyterian")), They proclaimed a devotion to hard work, temperance, frugality and upward mobility, with which historians today largely agree. A major Unitarian magazine, the *Christian Monthly Repository* asserted in 1827:

> Throughout England a great part of the more active members of society, who have the most intercourse with the people have the most influence over them, are Protestant Dissenters. These are manufacturers, merchants and substantial tradesman, or persons who are in the enjoyment of a competency realised by trade, commerce and manufacturers, gentlemen of the professions of law and physic, and agriculturalists, of that class particularly who live upon their own freehold. The virtues of temperance, frugality, prudence and integrity promoted by religious Nonconformity... assist the temporal prosperity of these descriptions of persons, as they tend also to lift others to the same rank in society.[[33]](#cite_note-38)

The Nonconformists suffered under a series of disabilities, some of which were symbolic and others were painful, and they were all deliberately imposed to weaken the dissenting challenge to Anglican orthodoxy.[[34]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDitchfield1974551–577-39) The Nonconformists allied with the Whigs to demand for civil and religious equality. Grievances included a 1753 law that to be legally recognised marriage had to take place in the [Anglican parish church](/wiki/Anglican_parish_church "Anglican parish church"). The Anglican [parish register](/wiki/Parish_register "Parish register") was the only legally accepted birth documentation. The Anglican parish controlled the only religious burial grounds. The [Universities of Oxford](/wiki/University_of_Oxford "University of Oxford") and [Cambridge](/wiki/University_of_Cambridge "University of Cambridge") had to reject non-Anglican applicants. At the local level, everyone who lived in the boundaries of an Anglican church was required to pay taxes to support the parish. The Test and Corporation laws required all national and local government officials had to attend Anglican church services. In February 1828, Whig leader [Lord John Russell](/wiki/Lord_John_Russell "Lord John Russell"), presented petitions assembled by the main Nonconformist pressure group, the United Committee, which represented Congregationalist, Baptists and Unitarians.[[35]](#cite_note-40) Their demand was the immediate repeal of the hated laws. Wellington and Peel originally were opposed, but then tried to compromise. They finally gave, splitting the Tory party, and signaling that the once unstoppable power of the Anglican establishment was now unexpectedly fragile and vulnerable to challenge.[[36]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMartin199664–66,_108-41)[[37]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBriggs1959250–251-42)

### Foreign policy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_Kingdom_of_Great_Britain_and_Ireland&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Foreign policy")]

Main article: [History of the foreign relations of the United Kingdom](/wiki/History_of_the_foreign_relations_of_the_United_Kingdom "History of the foreign relations of the United Kingdom")

Three men shaped British foreign policy from 1810 to 1860, with only a few interruptions, [Viscount Castlereagh](/wiki/Viscount_Castlereagh "Viscount Castlereagh") (especially 1812–1822). [George Canning](/wiki/George_Canning "George Canning") (especially 1807–1829) and [Viscount Palmerston](/wiki/Henry_John_Temple,_3rd_Viscount_Palmerston "Henry John Temple, 3rd Viscount Palmerston") (especially 1830–1865). For a complete list, see [Secretary of State for Foreign and Commonwealth Affairs](/wiki/Secretary_of_State_for_Foreign_and_Commonwealth_Affairs "Secretary of State for Foreign and Commonwealth Affairs").

The coalition that defeated Napoleon was financed by Britain, and held together at the [Congress of Vienna](/wiki/Congress_of_Vienna "Congress of Vienna") in 1814–1815. It successfully broke Napoleon's comeback attempt in 1815. Castlereagh played a central role at Vienna, along with Austrian leader [Klemens von Metternich](/wiki/Klemens_von_Metternich "Klemens von Metternich"). While many Europeans wanted to punish France heavily, Castlereagh insisted on a mild peace, with the [Kingdom of France](/wiki/Kingdom_of_France "Kingdom of France") to pay 700 million livre in indemnities and lose the territory seized after 1791. He realised that harsher terms would lead to a dangerous reaction in France, and now that the conservative old-fashioned [Bourbons](/wiki/Bourbons "Bourbons") were back in power, they were no longer a threat to attempt to conquer all of Europe. Indeed, Castlereagh emphasised the need for a "[balance of power](/wiki/Balance_of_power_(international_relations) "Balance of power (international relations)")", whereby no nation would be powerful enough to threaten the conquest of Europe the way Napoleon had.[[38]](#cite_note-43) Vienna ushered in a century of peace, with no great wars and few important localised ones until the [Crimean War](/wiki/Crimean_War "Crimean War") (1853–1856).[[39]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBlack200674–77-44) Prussia, Austria, and Russia, as [absolute monarchies](/wiki/Absolute_monarchies "Absolute monarchies"), tried to suppress liberalism wherever it might occur. Britain first took a Reactionary position at the [Congress of Vienna](/wiki/Congress_of_Vienna "Congress of Vienna") in 1815, but relented and broke ranks with the absolute monarchies by 1820. Britain intervened in Portugal in 1826 to defend a constitutional government there and recognising the independence of [Spain's American colonies](/wiki/Spanish_America "Spanish America") after their [wars of independence](/wiki/Spanish_American_wars_of_independence "Spanish American wars of independence") in 1824.[[40]](#cite_note-45) British merchants and financiers and, later, railway builders, played major roles in the economies of most Latin American nations.[[41]](#cite_note-46)

### Age of Reform

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_Kingdom_of_Great_Britain_and_Ireland&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Age of Reform")]

#### Main achievements

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_Kingdom_of_Great_Britain_and_Ireland&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Main achievements")]

In the 1825 to 1867 era, widespread public demonstrations, some of them violent, escalated to demand reform. The ruling Tories were dead set against anything smacking of democracy or popular rule and favoured severe punishment of demonstrators, as exemplified by the [Peterloo Massacre](/wiki/Peterloo_Massacre "Peterloo Massacre") in Manchester in 1819. The Tory ranks were cracking, however, especially when [Robert Peel](/wiki/Robert_Peel "Robert Peel") (1788–1830) broke away on several critical issues. Nevertheless, the Whig party gets most of the credit.[[42]](#cite_note-47)[[43]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBriggs1959256–343,_489–523-48)[[44]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWoodward196252–192-49) The middle classes, often led by [nonconformist](/wiki/Nonconformist_(Protestantism) "Nonconformist (Protestantism)") Protestants, turned against the Tories and scored the greatest gains. For example, symbolic restrictions on nonconformists called the [Test Acts](/wiki/Test_Acts "Test Acts") were [abolished in 1828](/wiki/Sacramental_Test_Act_1828 "Sacramental Test Act 1828"). Much more controversial was the repeal of severe discrimination against Roman Catholics after the Irish Catholics organised, and threatened rebellion, forcing [major concessions in 1829](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Relief_Act_1829 "Roman Catholic Relief Act 1829").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Financial reform, led by [William Huskisson](/wiki/William_Huskisson "William Huskisson") and Peel, rationalised the tariff system, and culminated in the great repeal of the tariffs on imported grain in 1846, much to the dismay of grain farmers. The 1846 repeal of the [Corn Laws](/wiki/Corn_Laws "Corn Laws") established free trade as the basic principle by which British merchants came to dominate the globe, and brought cheap food to British workers. A depoliticised civil service based on merit replaced patronage policies rewarding jobs for partisan efforts. Efficiency was a high priority in government, with the goal of low taxation. Overall, taxation was about 10%, the lowest in any modern nation.[[45]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETombs2014499-50)

Foreign policy became moralistic and hostile to the reactionary powers on the continent, teaming up with the United States to block European colonialism in the New World through the [Monroe Doctrine](/wiki/Monroe_Doctrine "Monroe Doctrine") of 1823. Slavery was abolished throughout the British Empire. The Royal Navy stepped up efforts to stop international trade in slaves.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Municipal reform was a necessity for the rapidly growing industrial cities still labouring under a hodgepodge of centuries-old laws and traditions. When Peel took over the [Home Office](/wiki/Home_Office "Home Office"), he abolished the espionage and cruel punishments, ended the [death penalty](/wiki/Capital_punishment_in_the_United_Kingdom "Capital punishment in the United Kingdom") for most crimes, and inaugurated the first system of [professional police](/wiki/Police "Police")—who in London to this day are still called "Bobbies" in his honour. The [Municipal Corporations Act 1835](/wiki/Municipal_Corporations_Act_1835 "Municipal Corporations Act 1835") modernised urban government, which previously had been controlled by closed bodies dominated by Tories. Over 200 old [municipal corporations](/wiki/Municipal_corporation "Municipal corporation") were abolished and replaced with 179 elected [borough councils](/wiki/Municipal_borough "Municipal borough"). Elections were to be based on registered voters, city finances had to be audited in a uniform fashion, and city officials were elected by the local taxpayers.[[46]](#cite_note-51)

By far the most important of the reforms was the democratisation of Parliament, which began in a small but highly controversial fashion with the [Reform Act 1832](/wiki/Reform_Act_1832 "Reform Act 1832"). The main impact was to drastically reduce the number of [very small constituencies](/wiki/Rotten_and_pocket_boroughs "Rotten and pocket boroughs"), with only a few dozen voters under the control of a local magnate. Industrial cities gained many of the seats but were still significantly underrepresented in Parliament. The 1831–1832 battle over parliamentary reform was, "a year probably unmatched in English history for the sweep and intensity of its excitement."[[47]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWebb1968198-52) Every few years an incremental enlargement of the electorate was made by Parliament, reaching practically all male voters by the 1880s, and all the women by 1928.[[48]](#cite_note-53) Both parties introduced paid professional organisers who supervised the mobilisation of all possible support in each constituency; about 80% of the men voted. The Tories discovered that their conservatism had an appeal to skilled workers, and also to women, hundreds of thousands of whom were organised by the [Primrose League](/wiki/Primrose_League "Primrose League").[[49]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETombs2014509-512-54) [Women's suffrage](/wiki/Women%27s_suffrage "Women's suffrage") was not on the agenda. The abolition of the [House of Lords](/wiki/House_of_Lords "House of Lords"), while often discussed, was never necessary because the upper house repeatedly retreated in the face of determined House of Commons action. After defeating the first two versions of the [Reform Act 1832](/wiki/Reform_Act_1832 "Reform Act 1832"), the Whigs got the king to agree to appoint as many new peers as was necessary to change the outcome. He promised to do so, but convinced the Lords it would be much wiser for them to approve the law.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

#### Political process

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_Kingdom_of_Great_Britain_and_Ireland&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Political process")]

A weak ruler as regent (1811–1820) and king (1820–1830), [George IV](/wiki/George_IV "George IV") let his ministers take full charge of government affairs. He was a deeply unpopular playboy. When he tried to get Parliament to pass a law allowing him to divorce his wife [Queen Caroline](/wiki/Caroline_of_Brunswick "Caroline of Brunswick"), public opinion strongly supported her.[[50]](#cite_note-55)

After four decades of rule by Pittites and Tories the first breakthrough in reform came in the removal by a Tory government of restrictions on the careers of Protestant Nonconformists in the repeal in 1828 of the laws that required Anglican church membership for many academic and government positions.[[51]](#cite_note-56) Much more intense was the long battle over the civil rights of Roman Catholics. [Catholic emancipation](/wiki/Catholic_emancipation "Catholic emancipation") came in 1829, which removed the most substantial restrictions on Roman Catholics in Great Britain and Ireland. The Duke of Wellington, as Tory prime minister, decided that the surging crisis in largely Catholic Ireland necessitated some relief for the Catholics, although he had long opposed the idea. The other main Tory leader was [Robert Peel](/wiki/Robert_Peel "Robert Peel"), who suddenly reversed himself on the Catholic issue and was roundly denounced and permanently distrusted by the [Ultra Tory](/wiki/Ultra-Tories "Ultra-Tories") faction of die-hards.[[52]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWoodward196276–77,_342–345-57)[[53]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBriggs1959195–200,_232–233-58)[[54]](#cite_note-59)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/25/Detail_House_of_Commons.JPG/500px-Detail_House_of_Commons.JPG)](/wiki/File:Detail_House_of_Commons.JPG)

*[The House of Commons, 1833](/wiki/The_House_of_Commons,_1833 "The House of Commons, 1833")* by [George Hayter](/wiki/George_Hayter "George Hayter") commemorates the passing of the Reform Act 1832. It depicts the first session of the newly reformed House of Commons on 5 February 1833. In the foreground, the leading statesmen from the Lords: [Charles Grey, 2nd Earl Grey](/wiki/Charles_Grey,_2nd_Earl_Grey "Charles Grey, 2nd Earl Grey") (1764–1845), [William Lamb, 2nd Viscount Melbourne](/wiki/William_Lamb,_2nd_Viscount_Melbourne "William Lamb, 2nd Viscount Melbourne") (1779–1848) and the Whigs on the left; and [Arthur Wellesley, 1st Duke of Wellington](/wiki/Arthur_Wellesley,_1st_Duke_of_Wellington "Arthur Wellesley, 1st Duke of Wellington") (1769–1852) and the Tories on the right.

[Earl Grey](/wiki/Charles_Grey,_2nd_Earl_Grey "Charles Grey, 2nd Earl Grey"), prime minister from 1830 to 1834, and his rejuvenated [Whig Party](/wiki/Whigs_(British_political_party) "Whigs (British political party)") enacted a series of major reforms: the [poor law](/wiki/English_Poor_Laws "English Poor Laws") was updated, [child labour](/wiki/Child_labour "Child labour") restricted and, most important, the [Reform Act 1832](/wiki/Reform_Act_1832 "Reform Act 1832") refashioned the British electoral system.[[55]](#cite_note-60) In 1832 Parliament abolished slavery in the Empire with the [Slavery Abolition Act 1833](/wiki/Slavery_Abolition_Act_1833 "Slavery Abolition Act 1833"). The government purchased all the slaves for £20,000,000 (the money went to rich plantation owners who mostly lived in England), and freed the slaves, most of whom were in the Caribbean sugar islands.[[56]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWoodward1962354–357-61)[[57]](#cite_note-62)

The Whigs became champions of parliamentary reform by making the [Reform Act 1832](/wiki/Reform_Act_1832 "Reform Act 1832") their signature measure. It sharply reduced the numbers of "rotten borough" and "pocket boroughs" (where elections were controlled by powerful families), and instead redistributed seats on the basis of population. It also broadened the franchise, adding 217,000 voters to an electorate of 435,000 in [England and Wales](/wiki/England_and_Wales "England and Wales"). The main effect of the act was to weaken the power of the [landed gentry](/wiki/Landed_gentry "Landed gentry"), and enlarge the power of the [professional](/wiki/Professional%E2%80%93managerial_class "Professional–managerial class") and [business middle-class](/wiki/Business_middle_class "Business middle class"), which now for the first time had a significant voice in Parliament. However, at this point the great majority of manual workers, clerks and farmers did not have enough property to qualify to vote. Many of them received the vote in 1867. The aristocracy continued to dominate the Church of England, the most prestigious military and naval posts, and high society, but not business, industry or finance. In terms of national governmental policy, the democratic wishes of the entire people had become decisive.[[58]](#cite_note-63)

Most historians emphasise the central importance of the legislation of the 1830s–60s, although there was a dissenting minority of scholars in the 1960s and 1970s who argued against deep meanings of Whiggish progress because each of the reforms was relatively minor in itself. Historian Richard Davis concludes that the scholarship of the 1970s represented "a vindication of the main outlines of the old "[Whig interpretation](/wiki/Whig_interpretation "Whig interpretation")." That is, the Reform Act 1832 was a response to mounting popular pressure. It was "the culmination of a long historical process, and an important turning point in the emergence of a more liberal and broadly based political system… it deserves its old designation of 'Great.'"[[59]](#cite_note-64)

David Thompson has stressed the revolutionary nature of the entire package of reforms:

> In all these ways—the organization of the new police (by Peel as Home Secretary in the 1820s), the new Poor Law, and in the new municipal councils—the pattern of government in England was changed fundamentally within a single decade. In conjunction with the removal of religious disabilities, these reforms laid the structural foundation for a new kind of State in Britain: a State in which the electoral rights and civil rights of citizens were extended and given greater legal protection, but in which the ordinary citizen was subjected to a much greater degree of administrative interference, direction, and control from the centre. The most spectacular element in this whole process—the Reform Bill of 1832—ensured that the state should also be partially democratized at the centre. The full significance of 1832 in the history of the country is appreciated only if it is seen as the central change in this mini-sided transformation of an agricultural nation ruled by squires, parsons, and the wealthy landowners into an industrial nation dominated by the classes produced by industrial expansion and commercial enterprise.[[60]](#cite_note-65)

#### Chartism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_Kingdom_of_Great_Britain_and_Ireland&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Chartism")]

Main article: [Chartism](/wiki/Chartism "Chartism")

[Chartism](/wiki/Chartism "Chartism") was a large-scale popular protest movement that emerged in response to the failure of the Reform Act 1832 to give the vote to the working class. It lacked middle-class support, and it failed repeatedly. Activists denounced the "betrayal" of the working classes and the "sacrificing" of their "interests" by the "misconduct" of the government. In 1838, Chartists issued the People's Charter demanding manhood suffrage, equal-sized election districts, voting by ballots, payment of Members of Parliament (so that poor men could serve), annual parliaments, and abolition of property requirements. The ruling class saw the movement as dangerous. Multiple large peaceful meetings across England demanded change but the Chartists were unable to force serious constitutional debate. In July 1839, however, the House of Commons rejected, by 235 votes to 46, a motion to debate the Chartists' national petition, bearing 1.3 million signatures.[[61]](#cite_note-66) Historians see Chartism as both a continuation of the 18th century fight against corruption and as a new stage in demands for democracy in an industrial society.[[62]](#cite_note-67)

### Prime ministers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_Kingdom_of_Great_Britain_and_Ireland&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Prime ministers")]

Prime ministers of the period included: [William Pitt the Younger](/wiki/William_Pitt_the_Younger "William Pitt the Younger"), [Lord Grenville](/wiki/Lord_Grenville "Lord Grenville"), [Duke of Portland](/wiki/Duke_of_Portland "Duke of Portland"), [Spencer Perceval](/wiki/Spencer_Perceval "Spencer Perceval"), [Lord Liverpool](/wiki/Lord_Liverpool "Lord Liverpool"), [George Canning](/wiki/George_Canning "George Canning"), [Lord Goderich](/wiki/Lord_Goderich "Lord Goderich"), [Duke of Wellington](/wiki/Duke_of_Wellington "Duke of Wellington"), [Lord Grey](/wiki/Charles_Grey,_2nd_Earl_Grey "Charles Grey, 2nd Earl Grey"), [Lord Melbourne](/wiki/Lord_Melbourne "Lord Melbourne"), [Lord Palmerston](/wiki/Lord_Palmerston "Lord Palmerston") and [Robert Peel](/wiki/Robert_Peel "Robert Peel").[[63]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECannon2002-68)[[64]](#cite_note-69)

The aristocracy remained dominant: there were 200 [hereditary peers](/wiki/Hereditary_peer "Hereditary peer") in the [House of Lords](/wiki/House_of_Lords "House of Lords") in 1860; by 1837 they numbered 428; in 1901, there were 592. The number rose to 622 by 1910. Reform legislation in 1832, 1867, 1884 and 1918 weakened the aristocracy in terms of its control of the House of Commons. However, it ran the government: of the ten prime ministers under Victoria, six were peers. The seventh was the son of a duke. Two (Peel and Gladstone) emerged from the business community and only one (Disraeli) was a [self-made man](/wiki/Self-made_man "Self-made man"). Of the 227 cabinet members between 1832 and 1905, 139 were sons of peers.[[65]](#cite_note-70)

[Field Marshal](/wiki/Field_marshal_(United_Kingdom) "Field marshal (United Kingdom)") [Arthur Wellesley, 1st Duke of Wellington](/wiki/Arthur_Wellesley,_1st_Duke_of_Wellington "Arthur Wellesley, 1st Duke of Wellington"), who defeated Napoleon, served as the leader of the Conservative party in the House of Lords, 1828–1846. Some writers have belittled him as a befuddled reactionary, but a consensus reached in the late 20th century depicts him as a shrewd operator who hid his cleverness behind the facade of a poorly-informed old soldier.[[66]](#cite_note-71) Wellington worked to transform the Lords from unstinting support of the Crown to an active player in political manoeuvring, with a commitment to the landed aristocracy. He used his London residence as a venue for intimate dinners and private consultations, together with extensive correspondence that kept him in close touch with party leaders in the Commons and with leading figures in the Lords. He gave public rhetorical support to [Ultra-Tory](/wiki/Ultra-Tory "Ultra-Tory") anti-reform positions, but then deftly changed positions toward the party's centre, especially when Peel needed support from the upper house. Wellington's success was based on the 44 peers elected from Scotland and Ireland, whose election he controlled.[[67]](#cite_note-72)

[Charles Grey, 2nd Earl Grey](/wiki/Charles_Grey,_2nd_Earl_Grey "Charles Grey, 2nd Earl Grey"), had promoted reform of Parliament since the 1790s, always to be defeated by the Ultra-Tories. The breakthrough came in his success in passage of the [Reform Act 1832](/wiki/Reform_Act_1832 "Reform Act 1832"). He sought this as the final step of reform, rather than a first step in a long process, emphasising the urgent need in 1832 to settle the intense and growing political unrest across Britain. He believed that the respectable classes deserved to have their demands for greater representation met, but he refused to extend political power to the mass of the lower middle class and working class, saying that they were not ready to be trusted with it. He wanted to preserve the basic elements of the existing constitution by removing obvious abuses, thinking that this would strengthen aristocratic leadership. He persuaded the king to promise to create enough new peers to force the bill through the House of Lords. The king made the promise while also advising the peers to stop blocking the bill. The Reform Act 1832 was Grey's principal achievement; it reflects his pragmatic, moderate and conservative character, as well as his parliamentary skills of timing and persuasion. His cabinet was a coalition of diverse interests, so in 1834 when it divided over the [Irish church](/wiki/Church_of_Ireland "Church of Ireland") question he resigned.[[68]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECannon2002436-73)[[69]](#cite_note-74)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/35/Lord-Palmerston-Addressing-The-House-Of-Commons-During-The-Debates-On-The-Treaty-Of-France-In-February-1860%2C-1863.jpg/250px-Lord-Palmerston-Addressing-The-House-Of-Commons-During-The-Debates-On-The-Treaty-Of-France-In-February-1860%2C-1863.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lord-Palmerston-Addressing-The-House-Of-Commons-During-The-Debates-On-The-Treaty-Of-France-In-February-1860,-1863.jpg)

[Lord Palmerston](/wiki/Lord_Palmerston "Lord Palmerston") addressing the House of Commons during the debates on the [Treaty of France](/wiki/Cobden%E2%80%93Chevalier_Treaty "Cobden–Chevalier Treaty"), February 1860

[Henry John Temple, 3rd Viscount Palmerston](/wiki/Henry_John_Temple,_3rd_Viscount_Palmerston "Henry John Temple, 3rd Viscount Palmerston"), played the dominant role in shaping British foreign-policy as Foreign Secretary (1830–1834, 1835–1841 and 1846–1851) and as prime minister (1855–1858, 1859–1865).[[70]](#cite_note-75) He served as [Secretary at War](/wiki/Secretary_at_War "Secretary at War") in Tory governments for two decades, but switched over to the Whig coalition in 1830. The Tories despised him thereafter as a turncoat, and many of the more radical Whigs were distrustful of his basically conservative views that saw him fainthearted about or opposed to reform measures. He typically warned on the one hand against delays and on the other hand against excessive enthusiasm for reforms, preferring compromise. He was keenly sensitive to public opinion, and indeed often shapes it through his dealings with newspaper editors.[[71]](#cite_note-76) When he sensed that public demand had reached an unstoppable momentum, he would work for a watered-down reform. He routinely gave the same advice to foreign governments. Diplomats across Europe took careful note of his move from the Tories to the Whigs, and suspected him of sympathy with the reform movements which were setting off upheavals in France, Belgium and elsewhere, and which frightened the reactionary governments of the major powers Russia, Austria and Prussia. In reality he drew his foreign policy ideals from Canning. His main goals were to promote British strategic and economic interests worldwide, remain aloof from European alliances, mediate peace in Europe and use British naval power sparingly as needed. He worried most about France as an adversary, although he collaborated with them as in securing the [independence of Belgium](/wiki/Independence_of_Belgium "Independence of Belgium") from the [Kingdom of the Netherlands](/wiki/Kingdom_of_the_Netherlands "Kingdom of the Netherlands").[[72]](#cite_note-77) He much preferred liberal and reform-oriented nations to reactionary powers. He placed a high priority on building up British strength in India, He spoke often of pride in British nationalism, which found favour in public opinion and gave him a strong basis of support outside Parliament.[[73]](#cite_note-78)[[74]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECannon2002719–720-79)

### Reformers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_Kingdom_of_Great_Britain_and_Ireland&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Reformers")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/11/Panopticon.jpg/250px-Panopticon.jpg)](/wiki/File:Panopticon.jpg)

[Jeremy Bentham](/wiki/Jeremy_Bentham "Jeremy Bentham")'s [panopticon](/wiki/Panopticon "Panopticon") prison (1791 drawing by [Willey Reveley](/wiki/Willey_Reveley "Willey Reveley"))

[Jeremy Bentham](/wiki/Jeremy_Bentham "Jeremy Bentham") (1748–1832) was an intellectual who focused on reforming [English law](/wiki/English_law "English law"). He was a leading promoter of [utilitarianism](/wiki/Utilitarianism "Utilitarianism") as a working philosophy of action. The "greatest happiness principle", or the [principle of utility](/wiki/Principle_of_utility "Principle of utility"), forms the cornerstone of Bentham's thought. By "happiness", he understood a predominance of "pleasure" over "pain". He is best known for his inspiration of the radical forces, helping them define those reforms that were most urgently needed and how they could be implemented. His intellectual leadership helped achieve many of the key legal, political, economic and social reforms of the 1830s and 1840s.[[75]](#cite_note-80) He especially influenced the reform of education, prisons, poor laws, legal procedures and parliamentary representation.[[76]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERobertsRobertsBisson2013[httpsbooksgooglecombooksidUoqTDAAAQBAJpgPA307_307]-81)

[John Bright](/wiki/John_Bright "John Bright") (1811–1889) built on his middle-class [Quaker](/wiki/Quaker "Quaker") heritage and his collaboration with [Richard Cobden](/wiki/Richard_Cobden "Richard Cobden") to promote all varieties of humanitarian and parliamentary reform. They started with a successful campaign against the Corn Laws. These were tariffs on imported food that kept up the price of grain to placate Tory landowners. The major factor in the [cost of living](/wiki/Cost_of_living "Cost of living") was the [price of food](/wiki/Food_prices "Food prices"), and the Corn Laws kept the price high. Bright was a powerful speaker, which boosted him to election to Parliament in 1843. His radical program included extension of the suffrage, land reform and reduction of taxation. He opposed factory reforms, labour unions and controls on hours For workers, women and children, arguing that government intervention in economic life was always mistaken. He opposed wars and imperialism. His unremitting hostility to the Crimean war led to his defeat for reelection in 1857. He was soon reelected from Birmingham, leading a national campaign for parliamentary reform to enlarge the suffrage to reach the working man. He was intensely moralistic and distrusted the integrity of his opponents. He loathed the aristocracy that continued to rule Britain. He held a few minor cabinet positions, but his reputation rests on his organising skills and his rhetorical leadership for reform.[[77]](#cite_note-82)

One historian summarised Bright's achievements:

> John Bright was the greatest of all parliamentary orators. He had many political successes. Along with Richard Cobden, he conducted the campaign which led to the repeal of the Corn Laws. He did more than any other man to prevent the intervention of this country (Britain) on the side of the South during the [American Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War"), and he headed the reform agitation in 1867 which brought the industrial working class within the pale of the constitution. It was Bright who made possible the Liberal party of Gladstone, Asquith and Lloyd George, and the alliance between middle-class idealism and trade unionism, which he promoted, still lives in the present-day Labour Party.[[78]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETaylor1953228-83)

## Victorian era

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_Kingdom_of_Great_Britain_and_Ireland&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Victorian era")]

Main article: [Victorian era](/wiki/Victorian_era "Victorian era")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e3/Queen_Victoria_by_Bassano.jpg/250px-Queen_Victoria_by_Bassano.jpg)](/wiki/File:Queen_Victoria_by_Bassano.jpg)

[Queen Victoria](/wiki/Queen_Victoria "Queen Victoria") reigned from 1837 to 1901.

The Victorian era was the period of [Queen Victoria](/wiki/Queen_Victoria "Queen Victoria")'s rule between 1837 and 1901 which signified the height of the British [Industrial Revolution](/wiki/Industrial_Revolution "Industrial Revolution") and the apex of the [British Empire](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire"). Scholars debate whether the Victorian period—as defined by a variety of sensibilities and political concerns that have come to be associated with the Victorians—actually begins with the passage of the [Reform Act 1832](/wiki/Reform_Act_1832 "Reform Act 1832"). The era was preceded by the [Regency era](/wiki/Regency_era "Regency era") and succeeded by the [Edwardian period](/wiki/Edwardian_period "Edwardian period"). Victoria became queen in 1837 at age 18. Her long reign saw Britain reach the zenith of its economic and political power, with the introduction of steam ships, railways, photography and the telegraph. Britain again remained mostly inactive in Continental politics.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The Queen played a small role in politics, but became the iconic symbol of the nation, the empire and proper, restrained behaviour.[[79]](#cite_note-84) Her success as ruler was due to the power of the self-images she successively portrayed of innocent young woman, devoted wife and mother, suffering and patient widow, and grandmotherly matriarch.[[80]](#cite_note-85)

### Foreign policy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_Kingdom_of_Great_Britain_and_Ireland&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Foreign policy")]

Main article: [International relations of the Great Powers (1814–1919)](/wiki/International_relations_of_the_Great_Powers_(1814%E2%80%931919) "International relations of the Great Powers (1814–1919)")

#### Free trade imperialism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_Kingdom_of_Great_Britain_and_Ireland&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Free trade imperialism")]

Main article: [Pax Britannica](/wiki/Pax_Britannica "Pax Britannica")

After the defeat of France in the [Revolutionary](/wiki/French_revolutionary_wars "French revolutionary wars") and [Napoleonic Wars](/wiki/Napoleonic_Wars "Napoleonic Wars") (1792–1815), the UK emerged as the principal naval and imperial power of the 19th century (with London the largest city in the world from about 1830).[[81]](#cite_note-86) [Unchallenged at sea](/wiki/Royal_Navy#1815–1914 "Royal Navy"), British dominance was later described as *[Pax Britannica](/wiki/Pax_Britannica "Pax Britannica")* ("British Peace"), a period of relative peace in Europe and the world (1815–1914).[[82]](#cite_note-87)[[83]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPorter1998[httpsbooksgooglecombooksidoo3F2X8IDeEC_332]-88) By the time of [the Great Exhibition](/wiki/The_Great_Exhibition "The Great Exhibition") of 1851, Britain was described as the "workshop of the world".[[84]](#cite_note-89) Using the imperial tools of free trade and financial investment,[[85]](#cite_note-90) it exerted major influence on many countries outside Europe and the empire, especially in [Latin America](/wiki/Latin_America "Latin America") and [Asia](/wiki/Asia "Asia"). Thus Britain had both a formal Empire based on British rule as well as an [informal one](/wiki/Informal_Empire "Informal Empire") based on the British pound.[[86]](#cite_note-91)

#### Russia, France and the Ottoman Empire

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_Kingdom_of_Great_Britain_and_Ireland&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Russia, France and the Ottoman Empire")]

One nagging fear was the possible collapse of the Ottoman Empire. It was well understood that a collapse of that country would set off a scramble for its territory and possibly plunge Britain into war. To head that off Britain sought to keep the Russians from occupying Constantinople and taking over the [Bosphorus Strait](/wiki/Bosphorus_Strait "Bosphorus Strait"), as well as from [threatening India via Afghanistan](/wiki/The_Great_Game "The Great Game").[[87]](#cite_note-92) In 1853, Britain and France intervened in the [Crimean War](/wiki/Crimean_War "Crimean War") against Russia. Despite mediocre generalship, they managed to capture the Russian port of [Sevastopol](/wiki/Sevastopol "Sevastopol"), compelling [Tsar Nicholas I](/wiki/Tsar_Nicholas_I "Tsar Nicholas I") to ask for peace.[[88]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFiges2012-93)

The [next Russo-Ottoman war](/wiki/Russo-Turkish_War_(1877%E2%80%931878) "Russo-Turkish War (1877–1878)") in 1877 led to another European intervention, although this time at the negotiating table. The [Congress of Berlin](/wiki/Congress_of_Berlin "Congress of Berlin") blocked Russia from imposing the harsh [Treaty of San Stefano](/wiki/Treaty_of_San_Stefano "Treaty of San Stefano") on the Ottoman Empire.[[89]](#cite_note-94) Despite its alliance with the French in the Crimean War, Britain viewed the Second Empire of [Napoleon III](/wiki/Napoleon_III "Napoleon III") with some distrust, especially as the emperor built up his navy, expanded his empire and took up a more active foreign policy.[[90]](#cite_note-95)

#### American Civil War

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_Kingdom_of_Great_Britain_and_Ireland&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: American Civil War")]

Main article: [United Kingdom and the American Civil War](/wiki/United_Kingdom_and_the_American_Civil_War "United Kingdom and the American Civil War")

During the [American Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War") (1861–1865), British leaders and elite favoured the [Confederate States of America](/wiki/Confederate_States_of_America "Confederate States of America"), a major source of [cotton](/wiki/Cotton_production_in_the_United_States "Cotton production in the United States") for textile mills. [Prince Albert](/wiki/Prince_Albert_of_Saxe-Coburg_and_Gotha "Prince Albert of Saxe-Coburg and Gotha") was effective in defusing a war scare in late 1861 over the [*Trent* Affair](/wiki/Trent_Affair "Trent Affair"). The mostly working-class British people generally favoured the [Union](/wiki/Union_(American_Civil_War) "Union (American Civil War)"). What little cotton was available came from [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City"), as the [Union blockade](/wiki/Union_blockade "Union blockade") of the Confederacy shut down 95% of Southern exports to Britain. Trade flourished with the Union and many young men crossed the Atlantic to join the [Union Army](/wiki/Union_Army "Union Army"). In September 1862, President [Abraham Lincoln](/wiki/Abraham_Lincoln "Abraham Lincoln") announced the [Emancipation Proclamation](/wiki/Emancipation_Proclamation "Emancipation Proclamation") would be issued in 90 days, thus making [abolition of slavery](/wiki/Abolition_of_slavery_in_the_United_States "Abolition of slavery in the United States") a war goal. Britain was long opposed to [slavery](/wiki/Slavery "Slavery"), itself having abolished it [some three decades earlier](/wiki/Slavery_Abolition_Act_1833 "Slavery Abolition Act 1833"), and any possibility of its intervention on behalf of the Confederacy ended.[[91]](#cite_note-96)

British companies built and operated fast [blockade runners](/wiki/Blockade_runners_of_the_American_Civil_War "Blockade runners of the American Civil War") to ship arms to and built warships for the Confederacy at considerable profit. London ignored American complaints that it allowed the selling of arms and building of warships for the Confederacy. Both blockade runners and warships caused a major diplomatic row that was partially resolved in the [*Alabama* Claims](/wiki/Alabama_Claims "Alabama Claims") in 1872, in the Americans' favour by payment of [reparations](/wiki/War_reparations "War reparations") only for damages caused by British-built Confederate warships.[[92]](#cite_note-97)

#### Empire expands

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_Kingdom_of_Great_Britain_and_Ireland&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Empire expands")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5b/Arthur_Mees_Flags_of_A_Free_Empire_1910_Cornell_CUL_PJM_1167_01_%28Reddit_source%29.jpg/500px-Arthur_Mees_Flags_of_A_Free_Empire_1910_Cornell_CUL_PJM_1167_01_%28Reddit_source%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Arthur_Mees_Flags_of_A_Free_Empire_1910_Cornell_CUL_PJM_1167_01_(Reddit_source).jpg)

The [British Empire](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire") in 1910

Starting in 1867, [Britain united most of its North American colonies as the Dominion of Canada](/wiki/Canadian_Confederation "Canadian Confederation"), giving it self-government and responsibility for its own defence, although Canada did not have an independent foreign policy until 1931. The second half of the 19th century saw a [scramble for Africa](/wiki/Scramble_for_Africa "Scramble for Africa") among the European powers. There was talk of war with France over the [Fashoda Incident](/wiki/Fashoda_Incident "Fashoda Incident") of 1898.

The rise of the [German Empire](/wiki/German_Empire "German Empire") after 1871 posed a new challenge, for it (along with the United States), threatened to usurp Britain's place as the world's foremost industrial power. Germany acquired a [number of colonies in Africa and the Pacific](/wiki/German_colonial_empire "German colonial empire"), but Chancellor [Otto von Bismarck](/wiki/Otto_von_Bismarck "Otto von Bismarck") succeeded in achieving general peace through his balance of power strategy. When [William II](/wiki/William_II,_German_Emperor "William II, German Emperor") became [German Emperor](/wiki/German_Emperor "German Emperor") in 1888, he discarded Bismarck, began using bellicose language, and planned to build a navy to rival Britain's.[[93]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETaylor1953Chapter_12-98) Britain realised its [isolation policy](/wiki/Splendid_isolation "Splendid isolation") was useless as large-scale alliances emerged. It restored good relations with France and the United States, and ended tensions with Russia, while the confrontation with Germany became a [naval race](/wiki/Anglo-German_naval_arms_race "Anglo-German naval arms race").

Ever since Britain had wrested control of the [Cape Colony](/wiki/Cape_Colony "Cape Colony") from the Netherlands during the [Napoleonic Wars](/wiki/Napoleonic_Wars "Napoleonic Wars"), it had co-existed with Dutch settlers who had migrated further away from the Cape and created two republics of their own: the [South African Republic](/wiki/South_African_Republic "South African Republic") and the [Orange Free State](/wiki/Orange_Free_State "Orange Free State"). The British imperial vision called for control over these new countries, and the [Afrikaans](/wiki/Afrikaans "Afrikaans")-speaking "[Boers](/wiki/Boers "Boers")" (or "[Afrikaners](/wiki/Afrikaners "Afrikaners")") fought back in the [War in 1899–1902](/wiki/Second_Boer_War "Second Boer War"). Outgunned by a mighty empire, the [Boer Commandos](/wiki/Boer_Commando "Boer Commando") waged a [guerrilla war](/wiki/Guerrilla_war "Guerrilla war") (which certain other British territories would later employ to attain independence). This gave the [British Armed Forces](/wiki/British_Armed_Forces "British Armed Forces") a difficult fight, but their weight of numbers, superior equipment and often brutal tactics, eventually brought about a British victory. The war had been costly in human rights and was widely criticised by Liberals in Britain and worldwide. However, the United States gave London its support. The [Boer republics](/wiki/Boer_republics "Boer republics") were merged with Cape Colony and Natal into the [Union of South Africa](/wiki/Union_of_South_Africa "Union of South Africa") in 1910; this had internal self-government, but its foreign policy was controlled by London and it was an integral part of the British Empire.[[94]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJuddSurridge2003-99)

### Leadership

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_Kingdom_of_Great_Britain_and_Ireland&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Leadership")]

Prime ministers of the period included: [Lord Melbourne](/wiki/Lord_Melbourne "Lord Melbourne"), [Robert Peel](/wiki/Robert_Peel "Robert Peel"), [Lord John Russell](/wiki/Lord_John_Russell "Lord John Russell"), [Lord Derby](/wiki/Lord_Derby "Lord Derby"), [Lord Aberdeen](/wiki/Lord_Aberdeen "Lord Aberdeen"), [Lord Palmerston](/wiki/Lord_Palmerston "Lord Palmerston"), [Benjamin Disraeli](/wiki/Benjamin_Disraeli "Benjamin Disraeli"), [William Ewart Gladstone](/wiki/William_Ewart_Gladstone "William Ewart Gladstone"), [Lord Salisbury](/wiki/Lord_Salisbury "Lord Salisbury") and [Lord Rosebery](/wiki/Lord_Rosebery "Lord Rosebery").

Disraeli and Gladstone dominated the politics of the late 19th century, Britain's golden age of parliamentary government. They long were idolised, but historians in recent decades have become much more critical, especially regarding Disraeli.[[95]](#cite_note-100)

#### Disraeli

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_Kingdom_of_Great_Britain_and_Ireland&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Disraeli")]

Main article: [Premierships of Benjamin Disraeli](/wiki/Premierships_of_Benjamin_Disraeli "Premierships of Benjamin Disraeli")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0c/Benjamin_Disraeli_by_Cornelius_Jabez_Hughes%2C_1878.jpg/250px-Benjamin_Disraeli_by_Cornelius_Jabez_Hughes%2C_1878.jpg)](/wiki/File:Benjamin_Disraeli_by_Cornelius_Jabez_Hughes,_1878.jpg)

[Benjamin Disraeli](/wiki/Benjamin_Disraeli "Benjamin Disraeli")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a7/Disraeli-1929-lobbycard.jpg/500px-Disraeli-1929-lobbycard.jpg)](/wiki/File:Disraeli-1929-lobbycard.jpg)
