[Lobby card](/wiki/Lobby_card "Lobby card") for the 1929 film *[Disraeli](/wiki/Disraeli_(1929_film) "Disraeli (1929 film)")*, showing actor [George Arliss](/wiki/George_Arliss "George Arliss"), who won an Oscar for his portrayal of the prime minister

[Benjamin Disraeli](/wiki/Benjamin_Disraeli "Benjamin Disraeli"), [prime minister in 1868 and 1874–80](/wiki/Premierships_of_Benjamin_Disraeli "Premierships of Benjamin Disraeli"), remains an iconic hero of the [Conservative Party](/wiki/History_of_the_Conservative_Party_(UK) "History of the Conservative Party (UK)"). He was typical of the generation of British leaders who matured in the 1830s and 1840s. He was concerned with threats to established political, social and religious values and elites; he emphasised the need for national leadership in response to radicalism, uncertainty and materialism.[[96]](#cite_note-101) Disraeli was especially noted for his enthusiastic support for expanding and strengthening the [British Empire](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire"), in contrast to Gladstone's negative attitude toward imperialism. Gladstone denounced Disraeli's policies of territorial aggrandisement, military pomp and imperial symbolism (such as making the Queen [Empress of India](/wiki/Empress_of_India "Empress of India")), saying it did not fit a modern commercial and Christian nation. However, Gladstone himself did not turn down attractive opportunities to expand the empire in Egypt.[[97]](#cite_note-102)

Disraeli drummed up support by warnings of a supposed Russian threat to India that sank deep into the Conservative mindset. His reputation as the "[Tory democrat](/wiki/One-nation_conservatism "One-nation conservatism")" and promoter of the [welfare state](/wiki/Welfare_state_in_the_United_Kingdom "Welfare state in the United Kingdom") fell away as historians showed that Disraeli had few proposals for social legislation in 1874–1880, and that the [Reform Act 1867](/wiki/Reform_Act_1867 "Reform Act 1867") did not reflect a vision of Conservatism for the unenfranchised working man.[[98]](#cite_note-103) However, he did work to reduce [class antagonism](/wiki/Class_conflict "Class conflict"), for as Perry notes, "When confronted with specific problems, he sought to reduce tension between town and country, landlords and farmers, capital and labour, and warring religious sects in Britain and Ireland—in other words, to create a unifying synthesis."[[99]](#cite_note-104)

In the popular culture, Disraeli was a great political hero, a status that persisted for decades after his death. For British [music hall](/wiki/Music_hall "Music hall") patrons in the 1880s and 1890s, "xenophobia and pride in empire" were reflected in the halls' most popular political heroes: all were Conservatives and Disraeli stood out above all, even decades after his death, while Gladstone was used as a villain.[[100]](#cite_note-105) After 1920, historical films helped maintain the political status quo by sustaining an establishment viewpoint that emphasised the greatness of monarchy, empire and tradition as they created "a facsimile world where existing values were invariably validated by events in the film and where all discord could be turned into harmony by an acceptance of the status quo."[[101]](#cite_note-106) Disraeli was an especially popular film hero: "historical dramas favoured Disraeli over Gladstone and, more substantively, promulgated an essentially deferential view of democratic leadership." Stage and screen actor [George Arliss](/wiki/George_Arliss "George Arliss") (1868–1946) was famous for his portrayals of Disraeli, winning the [Oscar](/wiki/Academy_Awards "Academy Awards") as best actor for 1929's *[Disraeli](/wiki/Disraeli_(1929_film) "Disraeli (1929 film)")*. Arliss "personified the kind of paternalistic, kindly, homely statesmanship that appealed to a significant proportion of the cinema audience… Even workers attending Labour party meetings deferred to leaders with an elevated social background who showed they cared.".[[102]](#cite_note-107)

#### Gladstone

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_Kingdom_of_Great_Britain_and_Ireland&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Gladstone")]

Main article: [Premierships of William Ewart Gladstone](/wiki/Premierships_of_William_Ewart_Gladstone "Premierships of William Ewart Gladstone")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c6/William_Ewart_Gladstone%2C_1892_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-William_Ewart_Gladstone%2C_1892_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:William_Ewart_Gladstone,_1892_(cropped).jpg)

Prime Minister [William Ewart Gladstone](/wiki/William_Ewart_Gladstone "William Ewart Gladstone")

[William Ewart Gladstone](/wiki/William_Ewart_Gladstone "William Ewart Gladstone") was the Liberal counterpart to Disraeli, serving as [prime minister four times (1868–1874, 1880–1885, 1886 and 1892–1894)](/wiki/Premierships_of_William_Ewart_Gladstone "Premierships of William Ewart Gladstone"). His financial policies, based on the notion of [balanced budgets](/wiki/Balanced_budget "Balanced budget"), low taxes and [laissez-faire](/wiki/Laissez-faire "Laissez-faire"), were suited to a developing capitalist society but could not respond effectively as economic and social conditions changed. Called the "Grand Old Man" later in life, he was always a dynamic popular orator who appealed strongly to British workers and the lower middle class. The deeply religious Gladstone brought a new moral tone to politics with his evangelical sensibility. His moralism often angered his upper-class opponents (including Queen Victoria, who strongly favoured Disraeli), and his heavy-handed control split the Liberal party. His [foreign policy](/wiki/Foreign_policy_of_William_Ewart_Gladstone "Foreign policy of William Ewart Gladstone") goal was to create a European order based on cooperation rather than conflict and mutual trust instead of rivalry and suspicion; the rule of law was to supplant the reign of force and self-interest. This Gladstonian concept of a harmonious Concert of Europe was opposed to and ultimately defeated by the Germans with a [Bismarckian](/wiki/Bismarckian "Bismarckian") system of manipulated alliances and antagonisms.[[103]](#cite_note-108)

#### Salisbury

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_Kingdom_of_Great_Britain_and_Ireland&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Salisbury")]

Conservative Prime Minister [Lord Salisbury](/wiki/Lord_Salisbury "Lord Salisbury") was a "talented leader who was an icon of [traditional, aristocratic conservatism](/wiki/Traditionalist_conservatism "Traditionalist conservatism")".[[104]](#cite_note-109) Salisbury was "a great foreign minister, [but] essentially negative, indeed reactionary in home affairs".[[105]](#cite_note-110) Another historian's estimate is more favourable; he portrays Salisbury as a leader who "held back the popular tide for twenty years."[[106]](#cite_note-111) "[I]nto the 'progressive' strain of modern Conservatism he simply will not fit."[[107]](#cite_note-112) One historian pointed to "the narrow cynicism of Salisbury".[[108]](#cite_note-113) One admirer of Salisbury agrees that Salisbury found the democracy born of the 1867 and 1884 Reform Acts as "perhaps less objectionable than he had expected—succeeding, through his public persona, in mitigating some part of its nastiness."[[109]](#cite_note-114)

### Morality

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_Kingdom_of_Great_Britain_and_Ireland&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Morality")]

Main article: [Victorian morality](/wiki/Victorian_morality "Victorian morality")

The Victorian era is famous for the Victorian standards of personal morality. Historians generally agree that the middle classes held high personal moral standards (and usually followed them), but have debated whether the working classes followed suit. Moralists in the late 19th century such as [Henry Mayhew](/wiki/Henry_Mayhew "Henry Mayhew") decried the slums for their supposed high levels of [cohabitation](/wiki/Cohabitation "Cohabitation") without marriage and [illegitimate births](/wiki/Illegitimate_birth "Illegitimate birth"). However, new research using computerised matching of data files shows that the rates of cohabitation then were quite low—under 5%—for the working class and the poor.[[110]](#cite_note-115)

## Early 20th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_Kingdom_of_Great_Britain_and_Ireland&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Early 20th century")]

Prime ministers from 1900 to 1923: [Lord Salisbury](/wiki/Lord_Salisbury "Lord Salisbury"), [Arthur Balfour](/wiki/Arthur_Balfour "Arthur Balfour"), [Henry Campbell-Bannerman](/wiki/Henry_Campbell-Bannerman "Henry Campbell-Bannerman"), [H. H. Asquith](/wiki/H._H._Asquith "H. H. Asquith"), [David Lloyd George](/wiki/David_Lloyd_George "David Lloyd George"), [Bonar Law](/wiki/Bonar_Law "Bonar Law").

### Edwardian era: 1901–1914

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_Kingdom_of_Great_Britain_and_Ireland&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Edwardian era: 1901–1914")]

Main article: [Edwardian era](/wiki/Edwardian_era "Edwardian era")

Queen Victoria died in 1901 and her son [Edward VII](/wiki/Edward_VII "Edward VII") became king, inaugurating the Edwardian era, which was characterised by great and ostentatious displays of wealth in contrast to the sombre Victorian Era. With the advent of the 20th century, things such as [motion pictures](/wiki/Motion_pictures "Motion pictures"), [automobiles](/wiki/Automobiles "Automobiles"), and [aeroplanes](/wiki/Aeroplanes "Aeroplanes") were coming into use. The new century was characterised by a feeling of great optimism. The social reforms of the last century continued into the 20th with the [Labour Party](/wiki/Labour_Party_(UK) "Labour Party (UK)") being formed in 1900. Edward died in 1910, to be succeeded by [George V](/wiki/George_V "George V"), who reigned 1910–1936. Scandal-free, hard working and popular, George V was the British monarch who, with [Queen Mary](/wiki/Mary_of_Teck "Mary of Teck"), established the modern pattern of exemplary conduct for [British royalty](/wiki/British_royalty "British royalty"), based on middle-class values and virtues. He understood the overseas Empire better than any of his prime ministers and used his exceptional memory for figures and details, whether of uniforms, politics, or relations, to good effect in reaching out in conversation with his subjects.[[111]](#cite_note-116)

The era was prosperous but political crises were escalating out of control. Multiple crises hit simultaneously in 1910–1914 with serious social and political instability arising from the Irish crisis, labour unrest, the [women's suffrage movements](/wiki/Women%27s_suffrage_in_the_United_Kingdom "Women's suffrage in the United Kingdom"), and partisan and constitutional struggles in Parliament. At one point it even seemed the Army might refuse orders dealing with Ireland.[[112]](#cite_note-117) No solution appeared in sight when the unexpected outbreak of the Great War in 1914 put domestic issues on hold. The political party system of the Edwardian era was in delicate balance on the eve of the war in 1914. The Liberals were in power with a [progressive alliance](/wiki/Gladstone%E2%80%93MacDonald_pact "Gladstone–MacDonald pact") of [Labour](/wiki/Labour_Party_(UK) "Labour Party (UK)") and, off and on, [Irish nationalists](/wiki/Irish_nationalists "Irish nationalists"). The coalition was committed to free trade (as opposed to the high tariffs the Conservatives sought), free collective bargaining for trades unions (which Conservatives opposed), an [active social policy that was forging the welfare state](/wiki/Liberal_welfare_reforms "Liberal welfare reforms"), and [constitutional reform to reduce the power of the House of Lords](/wiki/Parliament_Act_1911 "Parliament Act 1911"). The coalition lacked a long-term plan, because it was cobbled together from leftovers from the 1890s. The sociological basis was non-Anglicanism and non-English ethnicity rather than the emerging class conflict emphasised by the Labour Party.[[113]](#cite_note-118)

### Great War

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_Kingdom_of_Great_Britain_and_Ireland&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Great War")]

Main articles: [History of the United Kingdom during World War I](/wiki/History_of_the_United_Kingdom_during_World_War_I "History of the United Kingdom during World War I") and [Ireland and World War I](/wiki/Ireland_and_World_War_I "Ireland and World War I")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/49/Sherwood_Foresters_following_up_Germans_near_Brie_March_1917_IWM_Q_1863.jpg/250px-Sherwood_Foresters_following_up_Germans_near_Brie_March_1917_IWM_Q_1863.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sherwood_Foresters_following_up_Germans_near_Brie_March_1917_IWM_Q_1863.jpg)

Men of the [Nottinghamshire and Derbyshire Regiment](/wiki/Sherwood_Foresters "Sherwood Foresters") following up the Germans near [Brie](/wiki/Brie,_Somme "Brie, Somme"), March 1917

After a rough start Britain under [David Lloyd George](/wiki/David_Lloyd_George "David Lloyd George") successfully mobilised its manpower, industry, finances, empire and diplomacy, in league with the French and Americans, to defeat the [Central Powers](/wiki/Central_Powers "Central Powers").[[114]](#cite_note-119)[[115]](#cite_note-120)[[116]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeckett2006-121)[[117]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarwick1965-122) The economy grew by about 14% from 1914 to 1918 despite the absence of so many men in the services; by contrast the [German economy](/wiki/German_economy "German economy") shrank 27%. The Great War saw a decline in civilian consumption, with a major reallocation to munitions. The government share of GDP soared from 8% in 1913 to 38% in 1918 (compared to 50% in 1943).[[118]](#cite_note-123)[[119]](#cite_note-124) The war forced Britain to use up its financial reserves and borrow large sums from the U.S.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[Britain entered the war](/wiki/British_entry_into_World_War_I "British entry into World War I") to protect [Belgium](/wiki/Belgium "Belgium") from [German aggression](/wiki/German_invasion_of_Belgium_(1914) "German invasion of Belgium (1914)"), and quickly assumed the role of fighting the [Imperial German Army](/wiki/Imperial_German_Army "Imperial German Army") on the [Western Front](/wiki/Western_Front_(World_War_I) "Western Front (World War I)"), and dismantling the overseas German Empire. The romantic notions of warfare that everyone had expected faded as the fighting in France bogged down into [trench warfare](/wiki/Trench_warfare "Trench warfare"). Along the Western Front the British and French launched repeated assaults on the German trench lines in 1915–1917, which killed and wounded hundreds of thousands, but made only limited gains. By early 1916, with number of volunteers falling off, the government imposed [conscription in Britain](/wiki/Conscription_in_the_United_Kingdom "Conscription in the United Kingdom") (but was not able to do so in Ireland where nationalists of all stripes militantly opposed it) in order to keep up the strength of the [army](/wiki/British_Army_during_World_War_I "British Army during World War I"). Industry turned out munitions in large quantities, with many women taking factory jobs. The [Asquith government](/wiki/Asquith_coalition_ministry "Asquith coalition ministry") proved ineffective but when [David Lloyd George](/wiki/David_Lloyd_George "David Lloyd George") replaced him in December 1916 Britain gained a powerful and successful wartime leader.[[120]](#cite_note-125)

The Navy continued to dominate the seas, fighting the [Imperial German Navy](/wiki/Imperial_German_Navy "Imperial German Navy") to a draw in the only great battle, the [Battle of Jutland](/wiki/Battle_of_Jutland "Battle of Jutland") in 1916. [Germany was blockaded](/wiki/Blockade_of_Germany_(1914%E2%80%931919) "Blockade of Germany (1914–1919)") and was increasingly short of food. It tried to [fight back with submarines](/wiki/U-boat_campaign "U-boat campaign"), despite the risk of war by the powerful neutral power the United States. The waters around Britain were declared a war zone where any ship, neutral or otherwise, was a target. After the liner [*Lusitania* was sunk](/wiki/Sinking_of_the_RMS_Lusitania "Sinking of the RMS Lusitania") in May 1915, drowning over 100 American passengers, protests by the United States led Germany to abandon unrestricted submarine warfare. In spring 1917 it resumed the sinking of all merchant ships without warning. [The United States entered the war](/wiki/United_States_in_World_War_I "United States in World War I") alongside the Allies in 1917, and provided the needed manpower, money and supplies to keep them going. On other fronts, the British, French, New Zealanders, Australians and Japanese occupied Germany's colonies. Britain fought the Ottoman Empire, suffering defeats in the [Gallipoli Campaign](/wiki/Gallipoli_Campaign "Gallipoli Campaign") and (initially) in [Mesopotamia](/wiki/Mesopotamia "Mesopotamia"), while arousing the Arabs who helped expel the Turks from Mesopotamia and Palestine. Exhaustion and [war-weariness](/wiki/War-weariness "War-weariness") were growing worse in 1917, as the fighting in France continued with no end in sight. With Russia collapsing in the [1917 Revolutions](/wiki/Russian_Revolution "Russian Revolution") Germany now calculated it could finally have numerical superiority on the Western Front. The massive [German spring offensives](/wiki/German_spring_offensive "German spring offensive") of 1918 failed, and with arrival of a million of the [American Expeditionary Forces](/wiki/American_Expeditionary_Forces "American Expeditionary Forces") at the rate of 10,000 a day by May 1918, the Germans realised they were being overwhelmed. Germany gave up, agreeing to an [Armistice](/wiki/Armistice_of_11_November_1918 "Armistice of 11 November 1918") on 11 November 1918. It was actually tantamount almost to a surrender with Germany handing over her fleet and heavy weapons, and her army retreating behind the [river Rhine](/wiki/River_Rhine "River Rhine").[[121]](#cite_note-126)

By 1918, there were about five million people in the army and the fledgling [Royal Air Force](/wiki/Royal_Air_Force "Royal Air Force"), newly formed from the [Royal Naval Air Service](/wiki/Royal_Naval_Air_Service "Royal Naval Air Service") (RNAS) and the [Royal Flying Corps](/wiki/Royal_Flying_Corps "Royal Flying Corps") (RFC), was about the same size of the pre-war army. The almost three million casualties were known as the "lost generation", and such numbers inevitably left society scarred; but even so, some people felt their sacrifice was little regarded in Britain, with poems like [Siegfried Sassoon](/wiki/Siegfried_Sassoon "Siegfried Sassoon")'s *Blighters* criticising the war as a human failure. The literary legacy focused on mass death, mechanised slaughter, fallacious propaganda and deep disillusionment, thereby annihilating long-standing romanticised images of the glories of war.[[122]](#cite_note-127)

### Postwar

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_Kingdom_of_Great_Britain_and_Ireland&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Postwar")]

Main article: [Interwar Britain](/wiki/Interwar_Britain "Interwar Britain")

The war had been won by Britain and its allies, but at a terrible human and financial cost, creating a sentiment that wars should never be fought again. The [League of Nations](/wiki/League_of_Nations "League of Nations") was founded with the idea that nations could resolve their differences peacefully, but these hopes were unfounded.

Following the war, Britain gained the German colony of [Tanganyika](/wiki/Tanganyika_(territory) "Tanganyika (territory)") and part of [Togoland](/wiki/Togoland "Togoland") in Africa. Britain was granted [League of Nations mandates](/wiki/League_of_Nations_mandate "League of Nations mandate") over [Palestine](/wiki/Mandatory_Palestine "Mandatory Palestine"), which was turned into a [homeland](/wiki/Homeland_for_the_Jewish_people "Homeland for the Jewish people") for [Jewish settlers](/wiki/Aliyah "Aliyah"), and [Iraq](/wiki/Mandatory_Iraq "Mandatory Iraq"), created from the three [Ottoman provinces](/wiki/Ottoman_provinces "Ottoman provinces") in Mesopotamia; the latter of which became fully independent in 1932. The [Kingdom of Egypt](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Egypt "Kingdom of Egypt"), which had been [occupied by Britain since 1882](/wiki/Khedivate_of_Egypt "Khedivate of Egypt"), and a British [protectorate](/wiki/Protectorate "Protectorate") since 1914, became independent in 1922 after the [Egyptian Revolution of 1919](/wiki/Egyptian_Revolution_of_1919 "Egyptian Revolution of 1919"), although British troops remained stationed there until the [Suez Crisis](/wiki/Suez_Crisis "Suez Crisis") of 1956.

In domestic affairs the [Housing Act 1919](/wiki/Housing_Act_1919 "Housing Act 1919") led to affordable [council housing](/wiki/Council_housing "Council housing") which allowed people to move out of decrepit inner-city slums. The slums remained for several more years, with trams being electrified long before many houses. The [Representation of the People Act 1918](/wiki/Representation_of_the_People_Act_1918 "Representation of the People Act 1918") gave women householders the vote, but it would not be until 1928 that [full equal suffrage](/wiki/Universal_suffrage "Universal suffrage") was achieved. Labour displaced the Liberal Party for second place and achieved major success with the [1922 general election](/wiki/1922_United_Kingdom_general_election "1922 United Kingdom general election").[[123]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMedlicott1967Chapters_2–4-128)

## Ireland

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_Kingdom_of_Great_Britain_and_Ireland&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Ireland")]

Main articles: [History of Ireland (1801–1923)](/wiki/History_of_Ireland_(1801%E2%80%931923) "History of Ireland (1801–1923)") and [British rule in Ireland](/wiki/British_rule_in_Ireland "British rule in Ireland")

### Campaign for Irish Home Rule

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_Kingdom_of_Great_Britain_and_Ireland&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Campaign for Irish Home Rule")]

Main articles: [Great Famine (Ireland)](/wiki/Great_Famine_(Ireland) "Great Famine (Ireland)"), [Irish question](/wiki/Irish_question "Irish question"), and [Irish Home Rule movement](/wiki/Irish_Home_Rule_movement "Irish Home Rule movement")

Part of the agreement which led to the [1800 Act of Union](/wiki/1800_Act_of_Union "1800 Act of Union") stipulated that the [Penal Laws](/wiki/Penal_Laws_(Ireland) "Penal Laws (Ireland)") in Ireland were to be repealed and [Catholic emancipation](/wiki/Catholic_emancipation "Catholic emancipation") granted. However, [King George III](/wiki/King_George_III "King George III") blocked emancipation, arguing that to grant it would break his [coronation oath](/wiki/Coronation_oath "Coronation oath") to defend the [Anglican Church](/wiki/Anglican_Communion "Anglican Communion"). A campaign by the lawyer [Daniel O'Connell](/wiki/Daniel_O%27Connell "Daniel O'Connell"), and the death of George III, led to the concession of Catholic emancipation in 1829, allowing Roman Catholics to sit in the Parliament of the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland. Catholic emancipation was not O'Connell's real goal, which was the repeal of the act of union with Great Britain. On 1 January 1843 O'Connell confidently, but wrongly, declared that repeal would be achieved that year. When [potato blight](/wiki/Potato_blight "Potato blight") hit the island in 1846, much of the rural population, especially in Catholic districts, began to starve.[[124]](#cite_note-129)[[125]](#cite_note-130)

While government funds were supplemented by private individuals and charities, and aid from the United States, it was not enough to avert a major catastrophe. The government of [Lord John Russell](/wiki/Lord_John_Russell "Lord John Russell") attempted to raise a loan of £8 million and intended a further loan but this provoked a [financial crisis](/wiki/Financial_crisis "Financial crisis") made worse by demand for funds for railways and food imports.[[126]](#cite_note-131) The crisis prevented the expending of the loan if the pound was to remain convertible to gold and government funding was slashed in 1847 and the costs of relief transferred to local taxes in Ireland.[[127]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERead2022184–228-132) [Cottiers](/wiki/Cotter_(farmer) "Cotter (farmer)") (or farm labourers) were largely wiped out and [emigration](/wiki/Emigration "Emigration") soared during what is known in Ireland as the "[Great Hunger](/wiki/Great_Hunger "Great Hunger")".[[128]](#cite_note-133) A significant minority elected [Unionists](/wiki/Unionism_in_Ireland "Unionism in Ireland"), who championed the Union. A [Church of Ireland](/wiki/Church_of_Ireland "Church of Ireland") (Anglican) barrister [Isaac Butt](/wiki/Isaac_Butt "Isaac Butt"), built a new moderate nationalist movement, the [Home Rule League](/wiki/Home_Rule_League "Home Rule League"), in the 1870s. After Butt's death the home rule movement, or the [Irish Parliamentary Party](/wiki/Irish_Parliamentary_Party "Irish Parliamentary Party") as it had become known, was turned into a major political force under the guidance of [William Shaw](/wiki/William_Shaw_(Irish_politician) "William Shaw (Irish politician)") and a radical young [Anglo-Irish](/wiki/Anglo-Irish "Anglo-Irish") [Protestant](/wiki/Protestantism_in_Ireland "Protestantism in Ireland") landowner, [Charles Stewart Parnell](/wiki/Charles_Stewart_Parnell "Charles Stewart Parnell").[[129]](#cite_note-134)

Parnell's movement campaigned for "Home Rule", by which they meant that Ireland would govern itself as a region within the United Kingdom. Two home rule Bills (1886 and 1893) were introduced by the Liberal Prime Minister [William Ewart Gladstone](/wiki/William_Ewart_Gladstone "William Ewart Gladstone"), but neither became law, mainly due to opposition from the Conservative party and the [House of Lords](/wiki/House_of_Lords "House of Lords"). The issue was a source of contention throughout Ireland, as a significant majority of [Unionists](/wiki/Unionism_in_Ireland "Unionism in Ireland") (largely but not exclusively based in [Ulster](/wiki/Ulster "Ulster")), opposed home rule, fearing that a [Catholic Nationalist](/wiki/Rome_Rule "Rome Rule") ("[Rome Rule](/wiki/Rome_Rule "Rome Rule")") parliament in Dublin would discriminate or retaliate against them, impose [Roman Catholic doctrine](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_doctrine "Roman Catholic doctrine"), and impose tariffs on industry. While most of Ireland was primarily [agricultural](/wiki/Agriculture_in_Ireland "Agriculture in Ireland"), six of the counties in Ulster were the location of [heavy industry](/wiki/Heavy_industry "Heavy industry") and would be affected by any tariff barriers imposed.[[130]](#cite_note-bardon-135)

Irish demands ranged from the "repeal" of O'Connell, the "federal scheme" of [William Sharman Crawford](/wiki/William_Sharman_Crawford "William Sharman Crawford") (actually [devolution](/wiki/Devolution_in_the_United_Kingdom "Devolution in the United Kingdom"), not [federalism](/wiki/Federalism_in_the_United_Kingdom "Federalism in the United Kingdom") as such), to the [Home Rule League](/wiki/Home_Rule_League "Home Rule League") of Isaac Butt. Ireland was no closer to home rule by the mid-19th century, and rebellions in [1848](/wiki/Young_Irelander_Rebellion_of_1848 "Young Irelander Rebellion of 1848") and [1867](/wiki/Fenian_Rising "Fenian Rising") failed.[[131]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJackson2003-136)

O'Connell's campaign was hampered by the limited scope of the [franchise](/wiki/Suffrage "Suffrage") in Ireland.[[132]](#cite_note-137) The wider the franchise was expanded, the better anti-union parties were able to do in Ireland.[[133]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHoppen2000567-138) Running on a platform that advocated something like the [self-rule](/wiki/Self-rule "Self-rule") successfully enacted in Canada under the [British North America Act, 1867](/wiki/British_North_America_Act,_1867 "British North America Act, 1867"), home rulers won a majority of both county and borough seats in Ireland in 1874.[[133]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHoppen2000567-138) By 1882, leadership of the [home rule](/wiki/Home_rule "Home rule") movement had passed to Charles Stewart Parnell of the [Irish Parliamentary Party](/wiki/Irish_Parliamentary_Party "Irish Parliamentary Party"). A wider franchise also changed the ideological mix among non-Irish MPs, making them more receptive to Irish demands. The [1885 election](/wiki/1885_United_Kingdom_general_election "1885 United Kingdom general election") resulted in a [hung parliament](/wiki/Hung_parliament "Hung parliament") in which the Irish Parliamentary Party held the [balance of power](/wiki/Balance_of_power_(parliament) "Balance of power (parliament)"). They initially supported the [Conservatives](/wiki/Conservative_Party_(UK) "Conservative Party (UK)") in a [minority government](/wiki/Minority_government "Minority government"), but when news leaked that [Liberal Party](/wiki/Liberal_Party_(UK) "Liberal Party (UK)") leader Gladstone was considering home rule, the IPP ousted the Conservatives and brought the Liberals into office.[[134]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBiagini20109-139)

Gladstone's [First Home Rule Bill](/wiki/First_Home_Rule_Bill "First Home Rule Bill") was closely modelled on the self-government given [Canada in 1867](/wiki/Constitution_Act,_1867 "Constitution Act, 1867"). Irish MPs would no longer vote in Westminster but would have their own separate Dublin parliament, which would control domestic issues. Foreign policy and military affairs would remain with London.[[135]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKendle199245-140) Gladstone's proposals did not go as far as most Irish nationalists desired, but were still too radical for both [Irish unionists](/wiki/Irish_unionists "Irish unionists") and [British unionists](/wiki/British_unionists "British unionists"): his first home rule bill was defeated in the House of Commons following a split in his own party. Liberal leader [Joseph Chamberlain](/wiki/Joseph_Chamberlain "Joseph Chamberlain") led the battle against home rule in Parliament. He broke with Gladstone and in 1886 formed a new party, the [Liberal Unionist Party](/wiki/Liberal_Unionist_Party "Liberal Unionist Party"). It helped defeat home rule and eventually merged with the Conservative party. Chamberlain used [anti-Catholicism](/wiki/Anti-Catholicism_in_the_United_Kingdom "Anti-Catholicism in the United Kingdom") to build a base for the new party among "Orange" nonconformist Protestant elements in Britain and Ireland.[[136]](#cite_note-141) [Liberal Unionist](/wiki/Liberal_Unionist "Liberal Unionist") [John Bright](/wiki/John_Bright "John Bright") coined the party's catchy slogan, "Home rule means Rome rule."[[137]](#cite_note-142)

Gladstone took the issue to the people in the [1886 election](/wiki/1886_United_Kingdom_general_election "1886 United Kingdom general election"), but the Unionists (Conservatives plus the [Liberal Unionists](/wiki/Liberal_Unionists "Liberal Unionists")) won a majority. In 1890 a divorce case showed Parnell was an adulterer; he was forced from power, and died in 1891. Gladstone introduced a [Second Home Rule Bill](/wiki/Second_Home_Rule_Bill "Second Home Rule Bill") in 1893, which this time was passed by the Commons, but was defeated in the Conservative-dominated House of Lords.[[138]](#cite_note-143) The Conservatives came to power until 1906 and home rule became a dead issue, but the subsidised sale of farm land greatly reduced the Protestant presence in Ireland south of Ulster. Having been rejected by the Conservatives, the Irish nationalist forces had little choice but to support the minority Liberal Party. New groups split off and they finally all merged in 1900 into the Irish Parliamentary Party led by [John Redmond](/wiki/John_Redmond "John Redmond").[[139]](#cite_note-144)

The Conservative government also felt that the demands in Ireland could be satisfied by helping the Catholics purchase their farms from Protestant owners. A solution by money not force was called "killing home rule with kindness".[[140]](#cite_note-145) Reforms passed as a result included the [Local Government (Ireland) Act 1898](/wiki/Local_Government_(Ireland)_Act_1898 "Local Government (Ireland) Act 1898") and the [Land Purchase (Ireland) Act 1903](/wiki/Land_Purchase_(Ireland)_Act_1903 "Land Purchase (Ireland) Act 1903"). Between 1868 and 1908: spending on Ireland was generally increased, huge tracts of land were purchased from landlords and redistributed to smallholders, local government was democratised, and the franchise widely extended.[[141]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBoyce1995281–294-146) Ireland remained calm until the eve of the [First World War](/wiki/First_World_War "First World War"), when the Liberal government passed the [Government of Ireland Act 1914](/wiki/Government_of_Ireland_Act_1914 "Government of Ireland Act 1914") and Protestants in Ulster mobilised to oppose it by force.[[142]](#cite_note-147)

[Ulster Protestants](/wiki/Ulster_Protestants "Ulster Protestants") began to arm themselves and [form militias](/wiki/Ulster_Volunteers "Ulster Volunteers") ready to fight; senior leaders of the British army indicated they would not move to suppress the Protestants (the [Curragh incident](/wiki/Curragh_incident "Curragh incident")). Suddenly war with Germany broke out and home rule [was suspended](/wiki/Suspensory_Act_1914 "Suspensory Act 1914") for the duration. There was no conscription in Ireland; military service was optional. Large numbers of both Protestant and Catholic young men [volunteered to fight](/wiki/Kitchener%27s_Army "Kitchener's Army") Germany.

### Irish independence

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_Kingdom_of_Great_Britain_and_Ireland&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Irish independence")]

Main article: [Irish revolutionary period](/wiki/Irish_revolutionary_period "Irish revolutionary period")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/37/Republic_of_Ireland.svg/250px-Republic_of_Ireland.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Republic_of_Ireland.svg)

The Irish Free State (red) in 1922

The [Easter Rising](/wiki/Easter_Rising "Easter Rising") of 1916, using arms supplied by the [German Empire](/wiki/German_Empire "German Empire") was badly organised. The British army suppressed it after a week of fighting but the quick executions of 15 leaders alienated nationalist opinion. Overnight there was a movement away from home rule and toward Irish independence. The Cabinet decided that the 1914 Act should be brought into operation immediately and a Government established in Dublin.[[143]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJackson2003193–195-148) Negotiations were stalemated as Ulster mobilised. London made a second attempt to implement Home Rule in 1917, with the calling of the [Irish Convention](/wiki/Irish_Convention "Irish Convention"). Prime Minister Lloyd George sought a *dual policy* in April 1918 that attempted to link implementing Home Rule with extending conscription to Ireland. Irish nationalists rejected conscription and a wave of [anti-conscription demonstrations](/wiki/Conscription_Crisis_of_1918 "Conscription Crisis of 1918") signalled growing support for the demand for total independence.[[144]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJackson2003212–213-149) The old Irish Party collapsed and a new political force, [Sinn Féin](/wiki/Sinn_F%C3%A9in "Sinn Féin") which called for [force to achieve its goals](/wiki/Physical_force_Irish_republicanism "Physical force Irish republicanism"), united Irish nationalists.

Sinn Féin won the [1918 general elections](/wiki/1918_United_Kingdom_general_election_in_Ireland "1918 United Kingdom general election in Ireland") in Ireland and in keeping with their policy of [abstention](/wiki/Abstentionism "Abstentionism") did not send its elected MPs to Westminster, deciding to set up its own separatist parliament in Dublin; [Dáil Éireann](/wiki/D%C3%A1il_%C3%89ireann "Dáil Éireann"), which [declared independence](/wiki/Irish_Declaration_of_Independence "Irish Declaration of Independence"). The British government attempted to suppress the [First Dáil](/wiki/First_D%C3%A1il "First Dáil") and the [Irish War of Independence](/wiki/Irish_War_of_Independence "Irish War of Independence") followed. London's attempted solution was the establishment of two Irish parliaments to pave the way for the Fourth Home Rule Bill, enacted as the [Government of Ireland Act 1920](/wiki/Government_of_Ireland_Act_1920 "Government of Ireland Act 1920") while also attempting to defeat Sinn Féin and the [Irish Republican Army](/wiki/Irish_Republican_Army_(1919%E2%80%931922) "Irish Republican Army (1919–1922)") which by this time was operating under the remit of Dáil Éireann. In mid 1921 a truce was agreed between the British government and Sinn Féin and this resulted in the [Anglo-Irish Treaty](/wiki/Anglo-Irish_Treaty "Anglo-Irish Treaty"). On 6 December 1922, [Southern Ireland](/wiki/Southern_Ireland_(1921%E2%80%931922) "Southern Ireland (1921–1922)") formed a new [dominion](/wiki/Dominion "Dominion") named the [Irish Free State](/wiki/Irish_Free_State "Irish Free State"). As expected, "[Northern Ireland](/wiki/Northern_Ireland "Northern Ireland")" (six counties in [Ulster](/wiki/Ulster "Ulster")), immediately exercised its right under the Anglo-Irish Treaty to opt out of the new state. This treaty created a division in Irish nationalism and resulted in the [Irish Civil War](/wiki/Irish_Civil_War "Irish Civil War") between the [Provisional Government of Ireland](/wiki/Provisional_Government_of_Ireland "Provisional Government of Ireland") and the [Anti-Treaty faction of the Irish Republican Army](/wiki/Irish_Republican_Army_(1922%E2%80%931969) "Irish Republican Army (1922–1969)"). The union of Great Britain with most of Ulster was renamed the [United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland](/wiki/United_Kingdom_of_Great_Britain_and_Northern_Ireland "United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland") in 1927, and is known by this name to the present time.[[145]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJackson2003227–230-150)[[146]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMowat195557–108-151)

## List of monarchs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_Kingdom_of_Great_Britain_and_Ireland&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: List of monarchs")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c9/King_George_1923_LCCN2014715558_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-King_George_1923_LCCN2014715558_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:King_George_1923_LCCN2014715558_(cropped).jpg)

[George V](/wiki/George_V "George V"), the last British king to be styled as King of the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland

Further information: [History of monarchy in the United Kingdom](/wiki/History_of_monarchy_in_the_United_Kingdom "History of monarchy in the United Kingdom")

Until 1927, the monarch's royal title included the words "of the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland". In 1927, the words "United Kingdom" were removed from the royal title so that the monarch was instead styled as "King/Queen of Great Britain, Ireland...[and other places]". The words "United Kingdom" were restored to the monarch's title in 1953 with the reference to "Ireland" replaced with a reference to "Northern Ireland".[[147]](#cite_note-torrance-152)

* [George III](/wiki/George_III "George III") (1801–1820; monarch from 1760)
* [George IV](/wiki/George_IV "George IV") (1820–1830)
* [William IV](/wiki/William_IV "William IV") (1830–1837)
* [Victoria](/wiki/Queen_Victoria "Queen Victoria") (1837–1901)
* [Edward VII](/wiki/Edward_VII "Edward VII") (1901–1910)
* [George V](/wiki/George_V "George V") (1910–1922; title used until 1927 but remained monarch until his death in 1936)

## Notes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_Kingdom_of_Great_Britain_and_Ireland&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Notes")]

1. **[^](#cite_ref-1)** The state did not cease to exist after the [Irish Free State](/wiki/Irish_Free_State "Irish Free State") seceded from the Union in 1922 but continued as the same country, renamed under its current name of the "United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland" under [The Royal and Parliamentary Titles Act 1927](/wiki/Royal_and_Parliamentary_Titles_Act_1927 "Royal and Parliamentary Titles Act 1927").
2. **[^](#cite_ref-4)** Monarch of the separate Kingdoms of Great Britain and Ireland from 1760 until 1800.
3. **[^](#cite_ref-5)** Continued as monarch of the United Kingdom and the Irish Free State until 1936.
4. **[^](#cite_ref-6)** Prime Minister of Great Britain from 1783 until 1801. Prime Minister of the United Kingdom for a few months in 1801.
5. **[^](#cite_ref-7)** Became Prime Minister in October 1922. Continued until his resignation in 1923.
